texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Freshwater scarcity is one of the most pressing challenges today with up to four billion people living under water stress conditions for at least one month in a year [1], and it is expected to be exacerbated in the future because of climate change and population growth. Desalination has  evolved  into  an  alternative  water  supply  in  the  last  40  years  in response to the increasing freshwater scarcity. Desalinated water can be produced  by  using  brackish  water  (salt  content ≤ 10,000  mg/L)  or seawater (salinity of 25,000 45,000 mg/L). Brackish water is defined as water that exist naturally in the environment ( e.g. in estuaries and lakes) typically with a salt content of 1000 10,000 mg/L [2]. Given

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) is the key technology driving an energy-efficient and cost-effective desalination process. At the center of this technology are the thin film composite (TFC) membranes, which not only promise a stable operation but also high separation performances. The objective of this review is to consolidate recent advances in SWRO membranes from the standpoint of membrane materials, fabrication methodologies and applications. First, the thermodynamic limit and energy consumption of SWRO desalination are reviewed, before  we  discuss  the  current  status  of  SWRO  membranes,  highlighting  the  four  main  challenges  to  date -permselective tradeoff, relatively low single-pass boron rejection, membrane fouling and poor chlorine resistance. Thereafter, a comprehensive review of the membrane development is presented. We examine findings reported in research papers and patents, and various methods to achieve SWRO membranes of higher permselectivity,  boron  rejection,  and  chlorine  resistance  as  well  as  lower  fouling  propensity.  Key  insights  from  the membrane industry are also furnished. Then, we put together an outlook, featuring our perspectives on SWRO membrane development. Empirical data in this review are collated into an upper-bound relationship, which is tailored specifically for SWRO membranes and expected to provide benchmarking for future SWRO membrane development.  Since  the  traits  of  SWRO  membranes  are  unique  from  those  of  brackish  water  RO  (BWRO) membranes, we also distinguish the methods used for SWRO membrane fabrication to help zero in on the correct strategies, and provide insights for advancing new membrane designs. Overall, this review sums up the current state-of-the-art SWRO membranes, looking at the array of fabrication methods used thus far, and putting into perspective critical strategies to realize the next-generation TFC membranes that can address the future demands of SWRO and deliver a more competitive desalination process.
texts.text: Seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) is the key technology driving an energy-efficient and cost-effective desalination process. At the center of this technology are the thin film composite (TFC) membranes, which not only promise a stable operation but also high separation performances. The objective of this review is to consolidate recent advances in SWRO membranes from the standpoint of membrane materials, fabrication methodologies and applications. First, the thermodynamic limit and energy consumption of SWRO desalination are reviewed, before  we  discuss  the  current  status  of  SWRO  membranes,  highlighting  the  four  main  challenges  to  date -permselective tradeoff, relatively low single-pass boron rejection, membrane fouling and poor chlorine resistance. Thereafter, a comprehensive review of the membrane development is presented. We examine findings reported in research papers and patents, and various methods to achieve SWRO membranes of higher permselectivity,  boron  rejection,  and  chlorine  resistance  as  well  as  lower  fouling  propensity.  Key  insights  from  the membrane industry are also furnished. Then, we put together an outlook, featuring our perspectives on SWRO membrane development. Empirical data in this review are collated into an upper-bound relationship, which is tailored specifically for SWRO membranes and expected to provide benchmarking for future SWRO membrane development.  Since  the  traits  of  SWRO  membranes  are  unique  from  those  of  brackish  water  RO  (BWRO) membranes, we also distinguish the methods used for SWRO membrane fabrication to help zero in on the correct strategies, and provide insights for advancing new membrane designs. Overall, this review sums up the current state-of-the-art SWRO membranes, looking at the array of fabrication methods used thus far, and putting into perspective critical strategies to realize the next-generation TFC membranes that can address the future demands of SWRO and deliver a more competitive desalination process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At the core of SWRO is the membrane unit, which comprises semipermeable  membranes  capable  of  separating  salts  from  water.  In  a typical RO process, seawater is pressurized (~55 -70 bar) against the membrane that enables the selective transport of water over salt. As a consequence of the high pressure needed, the power consumption by the high-pressure pumps is the highest in an SWRO process. It is therefore imperative to develop robust ( i.e., mechanically stable) and high performance  SWRO  membranes  in  order  to  minimize  the  energy  consumption and capital cost of SWRO. For example, it was projected that tripling membrane permeability based on the current value of 1.0 L m 2 h 1 bar 1 could reduce the energy consumption and number of pressure vessels of SWRO plants by 15% and 44%, respectively, at a fixed recovery ratio and throughput [7].
texts.text: At the core of SWRO is the membrane unit, which comprises semipermeable  membranes  capable  of  separating  salts  from  water.  In  a typical RO process, seawater is pressurized (~55 -70 bar) against the membrane that enables the selective transport of water over salt. As a consequence of the high pressure needed, the power consumption by the high-pressure pumps is the highest in an SWRO process. It is therefore imperative to develop robust ( i.e., mechanically stable) and high performance  SWRO  membranes  in  order  to  minimize  the  energy  consumption and capital cost of SWRO. For example, it was projected that tripling membrane permeability based on the current value of 1.0 L m 2 h 1 bar 1 could reduce the energy consumption and number of pressure vessels of SWRO plants by 15% and 44%, respectively, at a fixed recovery ratio and throughput [7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The prevailing type of semi-permeable membrane adopted by commercial  SWRO  membrane  manufacturers  is  the  thin  film  composite (TFC)  polyamide  (PA)  membrane  capable  of  achieving  high  salt  rejections of 99.8% in standard SWRO test conditions (32,000 mg/L NaCl feed solution and applied pressure of 55 bar). TFC PA membranes are currently the gold standard for SWRO desalination because of its ability to  achieve  much  higher  water  permeability  and  salt  rejection  than asymmetric  cellulose  triacetate  membranes  invented  60  years  ago. Combined with their stability over a broad range of temperature and pH as  well  as  the  well-established  large  scale  fabrication,  TFC  PA  membranes have been in widespread use for SWRO desalination [8]. However, in spite of their excellent performance, TFC-SWRO membranes are still  facing  four  main  obstacles:  permselectivity  tradeoff,  insufficient boron  rejection,  membrane  fouling  and  low  tolerance  to  oxidizing agents such as chlorine. Therefore, it is reasonable to expect that future SWRO membrane research will be concentrated on these four areas to further improve the reliability and performance of SWRO membranes.
texts.text: The prevailing type of semi-permeable membrane adopted by commercial  SWRO  membrane  manufacturers  is  the  thin  film  composite (TFC)  polyamide  (PA)  membrane  capable  of  achieving  high  salt  rejections of 99.8% in standard SWRO test conditions (32,000 mg/L NaCl feed solution and applied pressure of 55 bar). TFC PA membranes are currently the gold standard for SWRO desalination because of its ability to  achieve  much  higher  water  permeability  and  salt  rejection  than asymmetric  cellulose  triacetate  membranes  invented  60  years  ago. Combined with their stability over a broad range of temperature and pH as  well  as  the  well-established  large  scale  fabrication,  TFC  PA  membranes have been in widespread use for SWRO desalination [8]. However, in spite of their excellent performance, TFC-SWRO membranes are still  facing  four  main  obstacles:  permselectivity  tradeoff,  insufficient boron  rejection,  membrane  fouling  and  low  tolerance  to  oxidizing agents such as chlorine. Therefore, it is reasonable to expect that future SWRO membrane research will be concentrated on these four areas to further improve the reliability and performance of SWRO membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For many years, membrane researchers have attempted to enhance the attractiveness of TFC PA membranes, with many works focusing on increasing  the  separation  performances  beyond  current  standards.
texts.text: For many years, membrane researchers have attempted to enhance the attractiveness of TFC PA membranes, with many works focusing on increasing  the  separation  performances  beyond  current  standards.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Often,  novel  approaches  to  improve  the  permselectivity  of  PA  membranes, such as the fabrication of an ultra-thin selective layer (~10 nm) [9], Turing structures [10], selective layer with precise molecular separation (~1 Å) [11] and many more, were of interest to the research community. While these efforts present paradigm shifts in membrane fabrication,  all  of  them  were  engineered  for  nanofiltration  (NF)  or brackish water RO (BWRO) applications under low applied pressures and low feed salinities (below 20 bar and 5000 mg/L respectively). For SWRO, the operating conditions are much harsher ( ≥ 32,000 mg/L feed salinity  with  applied  pressure  over  50 bar),  which  impose  extremely stringent requirements on the quality of membranes in terms of their high  salt  rejection  (typically  99.8%),  mechanical  strength  and  water transport  property.  Hence,  many  breakthroughs  in  PA  membrane fabrication cannot be applied for SWRO because these inventions lack the prerequisites needed for an SWRO process. This fact is further supported by the empirical data outlined in Fig. 1C and D which show that the  number  of  publications  and  patents  related  to  SWRO  membrane fabrication is much less than that of BWRO fabrication works [12].
texts.text: Often,  novel  approaches  to  improve  the  permselectivity  of  PA  membranes, such as the fabrication of an ultra-thin selective layer (~10 nm) [9], Turing structures [10], selective layer with precise molecular separation (~1 Å) [11] and many more, were of interest to the research community. While these efforts present paradigm shifts in membrane fabrication,  all  of  them  were  engineered  for  nanofiltration  (NF)  or brackish water RO (BWRO) applications under low applied pressures and low feed salinities (below 20 bar and 5000 mg/L respectively). For SWRO, the operating conditions are much harsher ( ≥ 32,000 mg/L feed salinity  with  applied  pressure  over  50 bar),  which  impose  extremely stringent requirements on the quality of membranes in terms of their high  salt  rejection  (typically  99.8%),  mechanical  strength  and  water transport  property.  Hence,  many  breakthroughs  in  PA  membrane fabrication cannot be applied for SWRO because these inventions lack the prerequisites needed for an SWRO process. This fact is further supported by the empirical data outlined in Fig. 1C and D which show that the  number  of  publications  and  patents  related  to  SWRO  membrane fabrication is much less than that of BWRO fabrication works [12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The focus of this paper therefore targets a holistic review of SWRO membrane  fabrication  works  in  order  to  facilitate  the  resolving  of various  challenges  in  SWRO  desalination.  Currently,  there  are  four major  membrane  manufacturers  supplying  commercial  SWRO  membrane  elements:  Toray  Industries  (Japan),  DuPont  Filmtec  (USA), Hydranautics (USA) and LG Chemicals (Korea). Although SWRO membranes are constantly evolving due to advancement by key membrane manufacturers, there have not been many reports on the progress of SWRO  membrane  development.  Understanding  the  pore  size  and morphology of the selective layer with valuable insights into the fine structural analysis of the protuberance structure of the PA layer will be of interest to the membrane industry [13,14]. Furthermore, due to the fact that SWRO membrane developments are mostly reported in patents, which are much less paid attention by the academic community, we attempted to incorporate SWRO membrane fabrication techniques from both research journal papers and patents to address this gap between conventional wisdom and reality. This review is expected to provide a big picture mapping for SWRO membrane researchers in academia and industry.
texts.text: The focus of this paper therefore targets a holistic review of SWRO membrane  fabrication  works  in  order  to  facilitate  the  resolving  of various  challenges  in  SWRO  desalination.  Currently,  there  are  four major  membrane  manufacturers  supplying  commercial  SWRO  membrane  elements:  Toray  Industries  (Japan),  DuPont  Filmtec  (USA), Hydranautics (USA) and LG Chemicals (Korea). Although SWRO membranes are constantly evolving due to advancement by key membrane manufacturers, there have not been many reports on the progress of SWRO  membrane  development.  Understanding  the  pore  size  and morphology of the selective layer with valuable insights into the fine structural analysis of the protuberance structure of the PA layer will be of interest to the membrane industry [13,14]. Furthermore, due to the fact that SWRO membrane developments are mostly reported in patents, which are much less paid attention by the academic community, we attempted to incorporate SWRO membrane fabrication techniques from both research journal papers and patents to address this gap between conventional wisdom and reality. This review is expected to provide a big picture mapping for SWRO membrane researchers in academia and industry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: including the energy consumption and thermodynamic limit of seawater desalination,  water  production  costs  and  fundamental  chemistry  of SWRO membranes. Then, the current challenges, including permeability-selectivity  tradeoff,  insufficient  boron  rejection,  poor chlorine  resistance  and  membrane  fouling,  encountered  in  SWRO membrane  fabrication  and  application  are  briefly  discussed.  Subsequently, the recent developments in SWRO membrane fabrication are reviewed  under  the  themes  of  enhancing  membrane  permselectivity through  the  optimization  of  the  support  and  selective  layer,  and methods to increase the boron rejection and chlorine resistance as well as fouling resistance. Whenever possible, the factors and mechanisms governing  the  fabrication  of  SWRO  membranes  are  compared  with BWRO membranes, so as to identify the key differences in fabrication approach for SWRO and BWRO membranes. In particular, the current status of SWRO membrane industry is highlighted. Finally, based on all the SWRO membrane performance data collated in this work, a new upper bound relationship specifically for SWRO membranes is proposed for future benchmarking. Prospective research needs and directions are also  recommended to  path  future  works  in  the  fabrication  of  SWRO membranes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Thermodynamic limit and energy consumption analysis of SWRO desalination
texts.text: 2.1. Thermodynamic limit and energy consumption analysis of SWRO desalination

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  amount  of  power  consumed  by  SWRO  plants  has  steadily decreased in the past 50 years (Fig. 2A) due to technological innovations such as the invention of high permeability membranes, highly efficient pumps and the usage of energy recovery devices [15]. In a typical SWRO desalination plant, the membrane unit itself takes up 70 85% of the overall  energy  consumption, with the other system components ( i.e., intake, pre/post-treatment and brine discharge) occupying the remaining  15 30% [15]. This makes the membrane unit the most energy intensive component of an SWRO desalination plant.
texts.text: The  amount  of  power  consumed  by  SWRO  plants  has  steadily decreased in the past 50 years (Fig. 2A) due to technological innovations such as the invention of high permeability membranes, highly efficient pumps and the usage of energy recovery devices [15]. In a typical SWRO desalination plant, the membrane unit itself takes up 70 85% of the overall  energy  consumption, with the other system components ( i.e., intake, pre/post-treatment and brine discharge) occupying the remaining  15 30% [15]. This makes the membrane unit the most energy intensive component of an SWRO desalination plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to thermodynamic principles, a desalination process will be the most efficient ( i.e., consumes minimum energy) if it occurs as a reversible  thermodynamic  process  [17],  regardless  of  the  separating mechanism or desalination technology employed. In theory, the minimum specific energy consumption ( SECmin ) of a reversible RO process is essentially the Gibbs free energy per unit volume of permeate [18]. From a thermodynamic standpoint, SECmin is the minimum amount of energy needed to reduce the entropy ( Δ S ) of the system from its initial to final state  ( i.e., seawater feed to  permeate and retentate  streams).  Mathematically, the relationship between SECmin ,  the free energy of mixing ( Δ G mix ) and the osmotic pressure of seawater ( π s ) is:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where Vw is the molar volume of water and nw is the number of moles of water. The theoretical minimum specific energy consumption ( SECmin ) of desalination (Fig. 2B) can be derived from the integration of Equation (1) [4]. According to the trend in Fig. 2B, it can be deduced that SECmin increases with the recovery and salinity of feed water. For example, the minimum energy required to desalinate brackish water and seawater at 50%  recovery  is  0.06  kWh/m 3 and  1.1  kWh/m 3 ,  respectively.  The 18-fold  higher  energy  required  to  desalinate  seawater  than  brackish water (at 50% recovery) reiterates the point that SWRO is an energy intensive process, notably because of the much higher osmotic pressure of seawater. In reality, the energy consumption of SWRO desalination plants are even higher because of its finite size and that the separation does not function perfectly as a reversible thermodynamic process.
texts.text: where Vw is the molar volume of water and nw is the number of moles of water. The theoretical minimum specific energy consumption ( SECmin ) of desalination (Fig. 2B) can be derived from the integration of Equation (1) [4]. According to the trend in Fig. 2B, it can be deduced that SECmin increases with the recovery and salinity of feed water. For example, the minimum energy required to desalinate brackish water and seawater at 50%  recovery  is  0.06  kWh/m 3 and  1.1  kWh/m 3 ,  respectively.  The 18-fold  higher  energy  required  to  desalinate  seawater  than  brackish water (at 50% recovery) reiterates the point that SWRO is an energy intensive process, notably because of the much higher osmotic pressure of seawater. In reality, the energy consumption of SWRO desalination plants are even higher because of its finite size and that the separation does not function perfectly as a reversible thermodynamic process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nanotechnology  opens  up  the  possibility  of fabricating nextgeneration desalination membranes with high intrinsic permeabilities using biological water channels or nanomaterials, such as aquaporins, synthetic  nanochannels,  nanotubes  and  two-dimensional  nanosheets, which boast superior transport properties [7,19,20]. The intrinsically high permeability of these nanomaterials have suggested the possibility of  fabricating  ultrahigh  permeable  membranes (UPMs) that are forecasted  to  have  2 3  times  higher  permeabilities  than  current  TFC membranes [7]. Empirical evidence has shown that a higher membrane permeability can indeed reduce the energy consumption of an RO process,  either  by  lowering  hydraulic  overpressure  needed  to  drive permeation at a fixed permeate flux or increasing the permeate flux at a fixed operating pressure [8]. However, in the past six years, some researchers claimed that the water permeability achievable by current RO membranes is already high enough given that the energy consumption is approaching performance limits set by external mass transfer rates and thermodynamics [7]. Hence, they argued that there is little incentives for developing UPMs because there is little room to further reduce the energy  consumption  of  SWRO  by  increasing  the  membrane  water permeability [21]. It is therefore intuitive to question whether UPMs as a strategy  can  be  leveraged  to  improve  the  energy  efficiency  and  reliability of SWRO, and most importantly to evaluate what future SWRO systems can and cannot achieve on the basis of UPMs.

The intrinsically high permeability of these nanomaterials have suggested the possibility of  fabricating  ultrahigh  permeable  membranes (UPMs) that are forecasted  to  have  2 3  times  higher  permeabilities  than  current  TFC membranes [7]. Empirical evidence has shown that a higher membrane permeability can indeed reduce the energy consumption of an RO process,  either  by  lowering  hydraulic  overpressure  needed  to  drive permeation at a fixed permeate flux or increasing the permeate flux at a fixed operating pressure [8]. Hence, they argued that there is little incentives for developing UPMs because there is little room to further reduce the energy  consumption  of  SWRO  by  increasing  the  membrane  water permeability [21]. It is therefore intuitive to question whether UPMs as a strategy  can  be  leveraged  to  improve  the  energy  efficiency  and  reliability of SWRO, and most importantly to evaluate what future SWRO systems can and cannot achieve on the basis of UPMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Also, to generate appropriate water fluxes, the applied pressure must be higher than that of the osmotic pressure of the concentrate. For a typical single-stage SWRO, this minimum energy consumption is equal to the applied pressure required to overcome the osmotic pressure of the concentrate stream [22]. At this stage, the system is said to be running at thermodynamic limit and the corresponding minimum energy is calculated to be 1.56 kWh/m 3 for seawater feed (35,000 mg/L) at a recovery of 50% [4]. It must be noted that membranes with higher permeability cannot reduce the energy consumption when the thermodynamic limit is reached because the energy consumption is fixed by the osmotic pressure of the concentrate stream.

texts.orig: lowest  energy consumption possible using higher permeability membranes. The modeling results from Werber et al. [23] and Cohen-Tanugi et al. [7] were selected and combined in Fig. 3B to give the general trend in SWRO and BWRO, respectively. For SWRO, the specific energy consumption (SEC) decreases steeply (0.25 kWh/m 3 ) when the membrane permeability increases from ~0.7 -2.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 . As the permeability increases from 2.0 to 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 , the SEC continues to decrease, albeit more gradually, and approaches an asymptote (~1.70 kWh/m 3 ) when the membrane permeability goes beyond 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 . These results point to the fact that membranes with permeabilities higher than 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 cannot reduce the energy consumption of SWRO further.
texts.text: lowest  energy consumption possible using higher permeability membranes. The modeling results from Werber et al. [23] and Cohen-Tanugi et al. [7] were selected and combined in Fig. 3B to give the general trend in SWRO and BWRO, respectively. For SWRO, the specific energy consumption (SEC) decreases steeply (0.25 kWh/m 3 ) when the membrane permeability increases from ~0.7 -2.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 . As the permeability increases from 2.0 to 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 , the SEC continues to decrease, albeit more gradually, and approaches an asymptote (~1.70 kWh/m 3 ) when the membrane permeability goes beyond 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 . These results point to the fact that membranes with permeabilities higher than 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 cannot reduce the energy consumption of SWRO further.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The difference between BWRO and SWRO trends in Fig. 3B can be attributed primarily to the higher osmotic pressure of seawater (Fig. 3A) which limits the amount of energy saved by UPMs in SWRO. The much lower osmotic pressure in BWRO leaves more room for energy reduction by UPMs with permeabilities > 6.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 . On top of that, the high salinity and high operating pressure in SWRO leads to more severe concentration  polarization  [7,24,25]  and  pressure  losses  [25]  as compared to BWRO. Here, our key message is that system thermodynamics,  concentration  polarization  and  irreversible  losses  impose  a constraint  on  the  potential  energy  savings  by  higher  permeability membranes, and this constraint is more significant for SWRO because of the  inherently  high  osmotic  penalty  involved.  On  the  basis  of  data depicted in Fig. 3B, the ideal SWRO membrane should have a membrane permeability of 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 with near perfect NaCl rejection ( ≥ 99.8%)  in  order  to  achieve  the  minimum  energy  consumption possible thermodynamically. It must be reiterated that whilst increasing membrane permeability (up to 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 )  can  potentially reduce  the  energy  consumption  of  SWRO,  a  high  salt  rejection  ( i.e ., lower salt permeability) is even more crucial for SWRO because of the high  salt  concentration  in  seawater  [5,13].  Hence,  the  99.8%  salt rejection  (high  selectivity)  is  an  uncompromising  trait  of  SWRO membranes.

On top of that, the high salinity and high operating pressure in SWRO leads to more severe concentration  polarization  [7,24,25]  and  pressure  losses  [25]  as compared to BWRO. Here, our key message is that system thermodynamics,  concentration  polarization  and  irreversible  losses  impose  a constraint  on  the  potential  energy  savings  by  higher  permeability membranes, and this constraint is more significant for SWRO because of the  inherently  high  osmotic  penalty  involved.  On  the  basis  of  data depicted in Fig. 3B, the ideal SWRO membrane should have a membrane permeability of 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 with near perfect NaCl rejection ( ≥ 99.8%)  in  order  to  achieve  the  minimum  energy  consumption possible thermodynamically. It must be reiterated that whilst increasing membrane permeability (up to 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 )  can  potentially reduce  the  energy  consumption  of  SWRO,  a  high  salt  rejection  ( i.e ., lower salt permeability) is even more crucial for SWRO because of the high  salt  concentration  in  seawater  [5,13].  Hence,  the  99.8%  salt rejection  (high  selectivity)  is  an  uncompromising  trait  of  SWRO membranes.

texts.orig: 2.2. Water production cost of SWRO desalination based on membrane
texts.text: 2.2. Water production cost of SWRO desalination based on membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The price of desalinated water has been decreasing throughout the past 20 years from approximately USD$ 2/m 3 in the 1990s to USD$ 1.1/ m 3 currently [15]. This is largely attributed to technological advancements ( e.g. , in membrane technology) that lowered the specific energy consumption of desalination as well as the construction of mega-sized SWRO plants that can produce permeate water at lower prices. It has been reported that the recently constructed large-scale SWRO plants in
texts.text: The price of desalinated water has been decreasing throughout the past 20 years from approximately USD$ 2/m 3 in the 1990s to USD$ 1.1/ m 3 currently [15]. This is largely attributed to technological advancements ( e.g. , in membrane technology) that lowered the specific energy consumption of desalination as well as the construction of mega-sized SWRO plants that can produce permeate water at lower prices. It has been reported that the recently constructed large-scale SWRO plants in

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the Middle East, Israel and Singapore can produce permeate water at ~USD$ 0.50/m 3 or less [5]. In the next couple of years, it is expected that the water production cost will not see a game-changing reduction given that SWRO is already a state-of-the-art desalination technology ( i. e. ,  no  impending technology breakthrough). However, it is projected that the water production cost could be gradually decreased by up to 20 -60% in the next 20 years through minor technological advances, continuous design innovations and optimization of SWRO plants [15].

texts.orig: 2.3. Basic structure and chemistry of the semi-permeable membrane
texts.text: 2.3. Basic structure and chemistry of the semi-permeable membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  TFC-SWRO  membrane  consists  of  three  layers  (Fig.  4A):  a polyester fabric for overall structural integrity (~120 μ m), a microporous  support  layer  (~50 μ m)  and  an  extremely  thin  selective  layer (100 300 nm) [12]. Polysulfone (PSf) is usually the choice of material for the support layer because of its chemical stability and resistance to compaction [12]. The PSf support is usually formed by a non-solvent induced  phase  separation  (NIPS)  technique,  in  which  the  dope  solution is immersed in a non-solvent bath to initiate phase inversion. The composition of the dope together with the phase inversion conditions can be fine-tuned to obtain the desired properties of the support layer in terms of the sublayer and pore characteristics [27].
texts.text: The  TFC-SWRO  membrane  consists  of  three  layers  (Fig.  4A):  a polyester fabric for overall structural integrity (~120 μ m), a microporous  support  layer  (~50 μ m)  and  an  extremely  thin  selective  layer (100 300 nm) [12]. Polysulfone (PSf) is usually the choice of material for the support layer because of its chemical stability and resistance to compaction [12]. The PSf support is usually formed by a non-solvent induced  phase  separation  (NIPS)  technique,  in  which  the  dope  solution is immersed in a non-solvent bath to initiate phase inversion. The composition of the dope together with the phase inversion conditions can be fine-tuned to obtain the desired properties of the support layer in terms of the sublayer and pore characteristics [27].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The prevalent method to fabricate the polyamide (PA) selective layer of SWRO membranes is interfacial polymerization (IP) (Fig. 4B). IP is based on the Schotten-Baumann reaction mechanism in which m -phenylenediamine (MPD) monomers migrate to the water/oil interface to crosslink with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) to form an ultrathin PA selective  layer  with  multiscale  heterogeneity  (Fig.  4C).  Scanning  transmission  electron  microscope  with  electron  energy  loss  spectroscopy (STEM-EELS) characterization revealed that the skin part of the protuberances is the real polyamide layer (~200 nm in height) that consist of a core polyamide layer (~20 nm in thickness) [13,14]. It must be highlighted that there exist a difference between the polyamide height and thickness, whereby the former depicts the visual height consisting of the protuberances (~200 nm) and the latter represents the core separation layer (~20 nm) embedded within the PA ridge and valley structure  [13,14].  In  a  typical  IP  process,  the  PSf  support  membrane  is immersed in an aqueous MPD solution so that amine monomers can penetrate into the pores of the support membrane. The excess MPD solution is then removed via a rubber roller or air knife, prior to contact with the organic phase consisting of TMC whereby the nascent selective PA layer is formed in situ on the support membrane. The TFC membrane is then subjected to a curing process (heat treatment at 50 100 ◦ C) to densify  and  stabilize  the  PA  layer.  Because  of  the  vigorous  and

Fig. 5B also shows the tradeoff relationship that is similar to the Robeson plot that has been widely used in field of gas separation membranes. On the basis of data depicted in Fig. 5, it is evident that the current commercial SWRO membranes can achieve a desalination performance of 1 2 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 with 99.7 99.9% NaCl rejection, which is consistent with the literature [8,38,39]. Out of the 28 SWRO data points summarized in Fig. 5A, it is noticed that 25 SWRO membranes can attain 99.8 -99.9% NaCl rejection in standard SWRO tests (with the remaining three exhibiting 99.7% NaCl rejection). It must be highlighted that the most of the latest products from commercial SWRO manufacturers are designed to attain ≥ 99.8% NaCl rejection [5] because of the crucial need for selectivity in SWRO [23]. Bearing in mind that the ideal performance of an SWRO membrane is 3.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 with ≥ 99.8%  NaCl  rejection  from  the  perspective  of  the  thermodynamic limit, the permeability of SWRO membranes can be improved further, on the condition that the selectivity is not compromised ( i.e., salt rejection is maintained at 99.8% or higher) [23].

Fig. 5B also shows the tradeoff relationship that is similar to the Robeson plot that has been widely used in field of gas separation membranes. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5. Insufficient boron rejection
texts.text: 2.5. Insufficient boron rejection

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a few decades, insufficient boron removal by RO membranes is one of  the  most  pressing  issues  for  SWRO  desalination.  Boron  exists ubiquitously in the lithosphere and hydrosphere in the form of complex compounds when combined with other elements such as oxygen. For example, the majority of boron exists in seawater as boric acid B(OH)3 with a typical concentration of ~4.5 mg/L [40]. The presence of boron is crucial for the healthy growth of plants and animals, and it is also an essential component for industries such as the microelectronics. However, boron can induce toxicity if present in excessive amounts, affecting plant cell growth and also endangering human health by damaging our cardiac and nervous systems [40]. Therefore, the concentration of boron in  drinking  water  has  been  subjected  to  strict  regulations  in  most countries,  with  a  concentration  of  0.5 2.4  mg/L depending on the regulatory guidelines stipulated by the authorities [41].
texts.text: For a few decades, insufficient boron removal by RO membranes is one of  the  most  pressing  issues  for  SWRO  desalination.  Boron  exists ubiquitously in the lithosphere and hydrosphere in the form of complex compounds when combined with other elements such as oxygen. For example, the majority of boron exists in seawater as boric acid B(OH)3 with a typical concentration of ~4.5 mg/L [40]. The presence of boron is crucial for the healthy growth of plants and animals, and it is also an essential component for industries such as the microelectronics. However, boron can induce toxicity if present in excessive amounts, affecting plant cell growth and also endangering human health by damaging our cardiac and nervous systems [40]. Therefore, the concentration of boron in  drinking  water  has  been  subjected  to  strict  regulations  in  most countries,  with  a  concentration  of  0.5 2.4  mg/L depending on the regulatory guidelines stipulated by the authorities [41].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The current state-of-the-art RO membranes cannot produce drinking water with such low concentrations of boron within a single pass. Based on 5 ppm of boron feed in standard SWRO test conditions, studies have shown that commercial SWRO membranes can reject at most 60 90% of boron by a single pass process [40,42], whereas it has been estimated that at least 90% boron rejection is needed to produce permeate water of drinking standard [43]. The ≥ 90% boron rejection is necessary to ensure that  the  boron  concentration in the permeate stream is ≤ 0.5 mg/L, which  is  in  accordance  to  the  provisional  guideline  set  forth  by  the World Health Organization [44]. As boron is present as boric acid, which has a small solute size and uncharged nature at natural pH, it can diffuse through  the  RO  membrane  easily  just  like  water  molecules,  thereby resulting in low boron rejection by SWRO membranes. So, to reduce the boron concentration to an acceptable level ( i.e., ≤ 0.5 mg/mL) [44], a two pass RO process (Fig. 6A) is usually employed in seawater desalination plants where part of the permeate from the first pass (SWRO) goes through a second pass RO [45 -47]. Whilst this method can reduce the boron concentration to the desired level, the additional pass will require higher  energy,  chemical  usage  as  well  as  capital  investments  [48]. Hence,  an  SWRO  membrane  with  high  boron  rejection  is  urgently needed for  its  potential  to  reduce  the  operating  and  capital  costs  of SWRO beyond what is achievable currently. The ideal SWRO membrane should have high boron rejection such that a single-pass SWRO unit is sufficient  for  the  separation  stage  (Fig.  6B).  The  current  strategies employed to increase boron rejection of SWRO membranes will be discussed thoroughly in Section 3.3.

The  current  strategies employed to increase boron rejection of SWRO membranes will be discussed thoroughly in Section 3.3.

texts.text: 2.6. Membrane fouling

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fouling in general refers to the temporal or permanent deposition of foulants on the membrane surface or within membrane pores. It is well-
texts.text: Fouling in general refers to the temporal or permanent deposition of foulants on the membrane surface or within membrane pores. It is well-

texts.orig: Fig. 6. (A) Partial two-pass RO configuration with an SWRO unit followed by a second-pass BWRO unit [47]. (B) Single-pass SWRO membrane unit [47]. The terms HP, BP and ERD corresponds to high-pressure pump, booster pump and energy recovery device, respectively.
texts.text: Fig. 6. (A) Partial two-pass RO configuration with an SWRO unit followed by a second-pass BWRO unit [47]. (B) Single-pass SWRO membrane unit [47]. The terms HP, BP and ERD corresponds to high-pressure pump, booster pump and energy recovery device, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: known that TFC-SWRO membranes have high fouling propensity due to their (a) inherent surface roughness (ridge and valley structure) that can easily trap macromolecules and colloids, (b) hydrophobic surfaces that draw and engage hydrophobic foulants via  hydrophobic  interactions [49],  and  (c)  negatively  charged  surface  carboxylic  groups  that  are vulnerable to direct bridging with cationic foulants. In SWRO, membrane fouling is a major challenge because it deteriorates the membrane performance and reduces its lifespan, inevitably leading to increased operation  and  capital  costs  [50,51].  Fouling  can  be  classified  into organic, inorganic, colloidal and biofouling [8,52]. Of these four types of fouling, biofouling is by far the most challenging for SWRO desalination primarily because of: i) the high bioufouling potential of the SWRO feed since the dissolved organic matter in seawater cannot be completely removed in pre-treatment stages and ii) the proliferative microorganisms that form mature biofilms due to the abundance of nutrients in the feed water [53,54]. Biofouling is caused by the undesired accumulation of  organisms  or  biopolymers  on  the  membrane  surface  eventually leading to the formation of biofilms (Fig. 7A). It is generally concluded that  the  water  transport  resistance  across  the  membrane  can  be increased because of biofilm formation, thereby leading to reduced flux (Fig. 7B). In addition, biofilms could hustle the enrichment of dissolved ions onto the PA surface, thereby exacerbating concentration polarization.  As  a  consequence  of  these  undesirable  effects,  a  higher  transmembrane pressure (TMP) (Fig. 7C) is needed to compensate for the increased  hydrodynamic  resistance  to  achieve  a  constant  flux  [55] which inevitably leads to greater energy consumptions. Alternatively, the  flux  would  decline  at  a  constant  TMP  because  of  the  additional resistance induced by the biofilm [56]. Empirical evidence from optical coherence  tomography  (OCT)  scans  suggests  that  the  continuous biomass accumulation can lead to the formation of a loose biomass film before  developing  into  a  double-layer  biofouling  morphology  [57] (Fig. 7D).

texts.orig: To  mitigate  membrane  fouling  (particularly  biofouling),  practical SWRO desalination industries currently employ low pressure membrane pre-treatment  using  microfiltration  (MF)  and  ultrafiltration  (UF)  to remove both dissolved and particulate matter in raw seawater before the SWRO stage [5,54]. However, MF/UF pre-treatment cannot completely remove all the microorganisms and thus the SWRO feed still has fouling potential despite the presence of pre-treatment stages [53]. Some researchers have also proposed the use of a tighter membrane such as nanofiltration (NF) for seawater softening as an alternative to MF/UF pre-treatment [58,59]. Empirical evidence suggests that NF pre-treatment can bring about various benefits, notably by: 1) lowering

texts.orig: SWRO membrane fouling propensity due to a reduction in the natural organic matter load and silt density index of the feed, 2) eliminating scaling  in  the  downstream  SWRO  membrane  due  to  the  removal  of hardness ions ( e.g. Ca 2 + , Mg 2 + ), and 3) lowering the osmotic pressure of the SWRO feed because of a reduction in the total dissolved solids (TDS) [60 -62]. While seawater pre-treatment using NF seems workable, the relatively higher capital and operating costs should be considered. The success of NF in replacing the conventional MF/UF pre-treatment will hinge on the former ' s ability to operate at low pressure with relatively high flux to minimize energy consumption and maximize productivity. Since the recovery of SWRO should not be limited by NF pre-treatment stage [63], the NF membrane should have high (UF-like) productivity in order  to  complement  the  downstream  SWRO  stage.  Specifically,  the ideal  NF  membrane  for  seawater  pre-treatment  should  have  high permeability ( > 20 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 ) at a UF-like operating pressure (~2 bar) [64]. Another crucial trait of the NF membrane is a high monovalent/divalent ion selectivity ( e.g. the  membrane  should  retain Mg 2 + /SO4 2 ,  but  allow  Na + /Cl to  readily  permeate through), which

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is worth noting that fouling is an inevitable issue that diminishes membrane  performance  and  thus  periodic  membrane  cleaning  is required to recover the decline in flux [66]. In SWRO desalination, a variety of methods are employed in membrane cleaning and the efficiency of these methods are usually measured by a flux recovery ratio (FRR) and foulant removal rate [67]. Typically, chemical cleaning is combined with physical cleaning to maximize the efficiency of foulant removal from the membrane surfaces. For example, chemical cleaning can be initiated to loosen the foulant layer by reducing the adhesion of foulants to the membrane surface. Thereafter, physical cleaning will be employed to accelerate the removal of the foulants via shear forces. For chemical cleaning, it is essential to consider the type of foulant, chemical properties  of  the  membranes  as  well  as  the  economics  from  a  cost perspective [67]. The common chemicals used in membrane cleaning include surfactants, acids and bases as well as chelating agents [68,69]. In some cases, these chemicals may produce undesired toxic byproducts and that chemical cleaning can also lead to shortened membrane lifespan. The development of anti-fouling membranes is thus crucial, for that  it  would  not  only  lead  to  tremendous  efficiency  gains,  but  also decrease  the  human  health  risks  associated  with  intensive  chemical usage. More importantly, it will decrease the frequency of the costly and energy intensive cleaning steps, reducing the downtime of the water treatment plants. It must be highlighted that membrane fouling is an issue that cannot be eliminated fully, but can be mitigated to a certain extent with fouling mitigation strategies in place. The fouling propensity of  any  membrane is intricately correlated  with  its  surface  properties (roughness,  charge  and  hydrophilicity)  [9,70].  Ideally,  the  selective layer should be smooth and hydrophilic to mitigate membrane fouling.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.7. Poor chlorine resistance
texts.text: 2.7. Poor chlorine resistance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The issue of biofouling in SWRO can be mitigated to a large extent if bactericides  and  water  disinfectants  (containing  chlorine  species  and oxidants) are used. However, the PA layer in TFC membranes is highly reactive towards oxidizing species and thus vulnerable to degradation by chlorine attack. The extremely low tolerance to chlorine ( < 0.1 ppm according to commercial SWRO membrane manufacturers) arises from the amide linkages that are susceptible to reaction by chlorine thereby altering the chemical structure of the PA layer and degrading the selective layer integrity. To develop RO membranes with higher chlorine resistance, it is essential to understand the fundamental mechanisms of chlorination. In aquatic chemistry, chlorine gas (Cl2) can form hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and hypochlorite ion (OCl ) in water which can further form other chlorine species depending on the prevailing conditions.  The  possible  ways  for  PA  degradation  due  to  chlorination  are summarized in Fig. 8.
texts.text: The issue of biofouling in SWRO can be mitigated to a large extent if bactericides  and  water  disinfectants  (containing  chlorine  species  and oxidants) are used. However, the PA layer in TFC membranes is highly reactive towards oxidizing species and thus vulnerable to degradation by chlorine attack. The extremely low tolerance to chlorine ( < 0.1 ppm according to commercial SWRO membrane manufacturers) arises from the amide linkages that are susceptible to reaction by chlorine thereby altering the chemical structure of the PA layer and degrading the selective layer integrity. To develop RO membranes with higher chlorine resistance, it is essential to understand the fundamental mechanisms of chlorination. In aquatic chemistry, chlorine gas (Cl2) can form hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and hypochlorite ion (OCl ) in water which can further form other chlorine species depending on the prevailing conditions.  The  possible  ways  for  PA  degradation  due  to  chlorination  are summarized in Fig. 8.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, the oxidizing species (HOCl) typically attack the amide linkage via a reversible N-chlorination reaction. This happens because the lone pair of electrons on the amidic nitrogen attracts attack from the partially positive electrophile Cl [73]. Because the N-chlorinated amide is unstable, Orton rearrangement occurs subsequently forming a ring-chlorinated product. It is noteworthy to mention that the Orton rearrangement  is  accelerated  under  pressurized  conditions  and  the transition from N- to ring-chlorinated product brings about maximum impairment  to  the  PA  selective  layer [74]. Alternatively, direct ring-chlorination can occur whereby chlorine attacks the aromatic ring via an electrophilic substitution reaction [74]. Regardless the mechanism, it is generally accepted that PA degradation due to chlorination and hydrolysis is predominant under an acidic and alkaline condition,
texts.text: First, the oxidizing species (HOCl) typically attack the amide linkage via a reversible N-chlorination reaction. This happens because the lone pair of electrons on the amidic nitrogen attracts attack from the partially positive electrophile Cl [73]. Because the N-chlorinated amide is unstable, Orton rearrangement occurs subsequently forming a ring-chlorinated product. It is noteworthy to mention that the Orton rearrangement  is  accelerated  under  pressurized  conditions  and  the transition from N- to ring-chlorinated product brings about maximum impairment  to  the  PA  selective  layer [74]. Alternatively, direct ring-chlorination can occur whereby chlorine attacks the aromatic ring via an electrophilic substitution reaction [74]. Regardless the mechanism, it is generally accepted that PA degradation due to chlorination and hydrolysis is predominant under an acidic and alkaline condition,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to Fig. 8, it is evident that the N- and ring-chlorination can disrupt  the  symmetry  of  the  PA  matrix,  hampering  the  formation  of intermolecular hydrogen bonds [75], which consequently increases the free-volume, flexibility and rotational freedom of the polymer chains. Resultantly, the water flux increases because the PA matrix is now looser due  to  the  disruption  in  crosslinking,  but  this  inevitably  causes  salt rejection to decrease concurrently. There are also some researchers who have instead reported a decrease in flux after chlorination [76]. They postulated that the loosely bound polymer matrix was more prone to compaction once pressure was applied, and consequently the compacted PA layer presented a larger hydrodynamic resistance which explained for the decrease in flux.
texts.text: According to Fig. 8, it is evident that the N- and ring-chlorination can disrupt  the  symmetry  of  the  PA  matrix,  hampering  the  formation  of intermolecular hydrogen bonds [75], which consequently increases the free-volume, flexibility and rotational freedom of the polymer chains. Resultantly, the water flux increases because the PA matrix is now looser due  to  the  disruption  in  crosslinking,  but  this  inevitably  causes  salt rejection to decrease concurrently. There are also some researchers who have instead reported a decrease in flux after chlorination [76]. They postulated that the loosely bound polymer matrix was more prone to compaction once pressure was applied, and consequently the compacted PA layer presented a larger hydrodynamic resistance which explained for the decrease in flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from the detrimental effect, the chlorination and oxidization process  in  SWRO  are  further  complicated  by  the  presence  of  other chemical  species  ( e.g. bromide  and  ammonia)  in  seawater  [77].  For example, chlorine and chloramines dosed during water disinfection are known to oxidize bromide into bromine, which will also partake in the degradation of the PA selective layer [78]. In summary, the concentration of chlorine in the seawater must be reduced to acceptable levels before feeding into the SWRO membrane unit because of the susceptibility of the PA selective layer to chlorine and oxidant attack. In the evaluation of the chlorination resistance of SWRO membranes, either passive or active chlorination tests (Fig. 8B) are adopted. In the former testing  protocol,  both  the  support  and  PA  layers  are  contacted  with chlorine whereas chlorine can only contact the PA layer on the feed side for the latter protocol. The current development of chlorine resistant SWRO membranes will be discussed in Section 3.5.

texts.orig: 3.1. Optimization of support layers
texts.text: 3.1. Optimization of support layers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most  research  works  in  SWRO  membrane  fabrication  focus  on making a selective layer with better permselectivity. This is attributed to the general belief that the permselectivity of a TFC membrane is predominantly controlled by the selective layer whereas the support layer provides  the  mechanical  strength  required.  Whilst  it  is  true  that  the selective layer plays a crucial role in determining the permselectivity of TFC membranes, due credit must also be given to the support layer for its role in forming an integral selective layer and ultimately a high performance TFC-SWRO membrane. In general, there are two important properties of the support layer (surface and sublayer) that need to be considered in the fabrication of SWRO membranes (refer to Table 1).
texts.text: Most  research  works  in  SWRO  membrane  fabrication  focus  on making a selective layer with better permselectivity. This is attributed to the general belief that the permselectivity of a TFC membrane is predominantly controlled by the selective layer whereas the support layer provides  the  mechanical  strength  required.  Whilst  it  is  true  that  the selective layer plays a crucial role in determining the permselectivity of TFC membranes, due credit must also be given to the support layer for its role in forming an integral selective layer and ultimately a high performance TFC-SWRO membrane. In general, there are two important properties of the support layer (surface and sublayer) that need to be considered in the fabrication of SWRO membranes (refer to Table 1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Surface properties. The support layer is the  platform  where  IP occurs and thus the surface properties of the support have an instrumental effect on the selective layer formation. Because the surface pore size  and  porosity  of  the  support  control  the  extent  and  rate  of  MPD monomer diffusion towards the organic phase, the formation of an integral and defect-free PA layer is contingent on the surface properties of the support. For instance, extremely small surface pores can lead to the formation of an imperfect selective layer because of insufficient MPD monomers impregnated within the pores [82]. However, the surface pores cannot be too big such that the PA layer sags deeply into the pores, leading to an unstable and non-uniform selective layer [80,86] that is more vulnerable to defect formation. According to SWRO research papers  [70,81,83]  and  patents  [80,87],  the  support  layer  pore  size  of 10 -70 nm is optimal to forming a defect-free PA layer.
texts.text: · Surface properties. The support layer is the  platform  where  IP occurs and thus the surface properties of the support have an instrumental effect on the selective layer formation. Because the surface pore size  and  porosity  of  the  support  control  the  extent  and  rate  of  MPD monomer diffusion towards the organic phase, the formation of an integral and defect-free PA layer is contingent on the surface properties of the support. For instance, extremely small surface pores can lead to the formation of an imperfect selective layer because of insufficient MPD monomers impregnated within the pores [82]. However, the surface pores cannot be too big such that the PA layer sags deeply into the pores, leading to an unstable and non-uniform selective layer [80,86] that is more vulnerable to defect formation. According to SWRO research papers  [70,81,83]  and  patents  [80,87],  the  support  layer  pore  size  of 10 -70 nm is optimal to forming a defect-free PA layer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  membrane  properties  as  well  as  the  rate  of  water  molecules transport across the selective layer are closely knitted to  the surface properties of the support, even for a fully defect-free TFC-SWRO membrane  [88]. In addition, it must be highlighted that other surface properties besides pore characteristics ( e.g. functional groups present, surface charge and hydrophilicity) are also important optimization parameters [70,79,80]. These surface properties affect the IP mechanism and dictate the crosslinking degree, thickness and roughness of the selective layer [70].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Sublayer structure. Because of the notably higher applied pressure (~55 bar) of SWRO as compared to BWRO ( < 20 bar), the support layer must deliver the mechanical strength and durability required to handle the harsh conditions of SWRO. For this reason, the sublayer structure of commercial SWRO membranes are typically tighter (refer to Fig. 9C -E), either as a full sponge-like structure or a partial sponge-like structure with only small macrovoids [83,89,90]. Alternatively, a more porous support can also be used for TFC-SWRO membrane if it has a recurring finger-like sublayer structure, such that the stresses are able to even out on the support [83]. In general, the tensile strength and modulus of SWRO membranes are in the range of 1.8 -5.0 MPa and 100 -190 MPa, respectively [83,89].
texts.text: · Sublayer structure. Because of the notably higher applied pressure (~55 bar) of SWRO as compared to BWRO ( < 20 bar), the support layer must deliver the mechanical strength and durability required to handle the harsh conditions of SWRO. For this reason, the sublayer structure of commercial SWRO membranes are typically tighter (refer to Fig. 9C -E), either as a full sponge-like structure or a partial sponge-like structure with only small macrovoids [83,89,90]. Alternatively, a more porous support can also be used for TFC-SWRO membrane if it has a recurring finger-like sublayer structure, such that the stresses are able to even out on the support [83]. In general, the tensile strength and modulus of SWRO membranes are in the range of 1.8 -5.0 MPa and 100 -190 MPa, respectively [83,89].

texts.orig: Since  both  MPD  and  TMC  comprise  a  benzene  ring,  the  product prepared from a reaction between these two compounds is commonly known as a fully aromatic PA. It is well established that the MPD/TMC chemistry produces a highly crosslinked PA selective layer which is the prerequisite to achieve high salt rejections in SWRO [38]. Indeed, this type of chemistry has been widely used in the fabrication of commercial SWRO membranes [12]. The ability to form a highly crosslinked PA is manifested by the chemical structure of MPD and TMC itself. First, the high electron density of the benzene ring make the amine functional groups of MPD highly reactive [38], which increases the rate and extent of IP reaction. The symmetry and steric openness of TMC can provide the optimized PA structure when reacted with aromatic amines [91]. These

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally,  from  SWRO  membrane  patents,  we  categorized  four important  parameters  affecting  the  permselectivity  of  SWRO  membranes: (1) thickness [93], (2) roughness [104], (3) crosslinking degree [106], and (4) hydrophilicity of the PA layer (refer to Fig. 10). First, the concentration of monomers is crucial given that it directly influences the rate and extent of the IP reaction [95]. Generally, there exists an optimal stoichiometry  for  the  preparation  of  high  permselectivity  RO  membranes  [38].  According  to  technical  disclosures  in  SWRO  membrane patents (Table 2), an amine to acid chloride weight ratio of 8 30 is optimal  [107],  presumably  to  facilitate  the  formation  of  a  highly crosslinked  PA.  Second,  in  the  fabrication  of  BWRO  membranes,  a relatively short reaction time ( ≤ 10 s) for IP is adopted at times, presumably to fabricate a thinner and less compact PA layer with the aim of achieving a higher water flux [40]. However, this approach might not be feasible for SWRO membrane fabrication because a thicker and more crosslinked PA layer is needed to achieve the salt rejection requirement of > 99%  [95].  Hence,  the  optimization  of  IP  conditions  to  obtain appropriate PA thickness for SWRO application is necessary. According to SWRO membrane patents, a reaction time of 1 min is essential to fabricate a thicker and more compact PA layer to attain the required > 99% salt rejection under high salinity conditions.

First, the concentration of monomers is crucial given that it directly influences the rate and extent of the IP reaction [95]. Generally, there exists an optimal stoichiometry  for  the  preparation  of  high  permselectivity  RO  membranes  [38].  According  to  technical  disclosures  in  SWRO  membrane patents (Table 2), an amine to acid chloride weight ratio of 8 30 is optimal  [107],  presumably  to  facilitate  the  formation  of  a  highly crosslinked  PA.  Second,  in  the  fabrication  of  BWRO  membranes,  a relatively short reaction time ( ≤ 10 s) for IP is adopted at times, presumably to fabricate a thinner and less compact PA layer with the aim of achieving a higher water flux [40]. However, this approach might not be feasible for SWRO membrane fabrication because a thicker and more crosslinked PA layer is needed to achieve the salt rejection requirement of > 99%  [95].  Hence,  the  optimization  of  IP  conditions  to  obtain appropriate PA thickness for SWRO application is necessary. According to SWRO membrane patents, a reaction time of 1 min is essential to fabricate a thicker and more compact PA layer to attain the required > 99% salt rejection under high salinity conditions.

Being  an  acid acceptor,  TEA  can  accelerate  the  IP  reaction  by  neutralizing  the hydrogen chloride produced during PA synthesis thereby increasing the rate and extent of PA formation according to Le Chatelier ' s principle [95,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 100,102]. Empirical evidence has shown that an aqueous solution pH of 9 -10 [95] is optimal for the formation of a highly dense and crosslinked selective layer. It is also worthy to note that a slightly 'looser ' active layer can be adopted in the fabrication of BWRO membranes in a bid to achieve higher flux [108]. However, this approach must be carefully dealt  with  for  SWRO  membranes  because  a  slight  decrease  in  crosslinking degree can result in a huge drop in salt rejection [95], rendering it impractical for SWRO taking into account the high requirement for salt rejection.

It is also worthy to note that a slightly 'looser ' active layer can be adopted in the fabrication of BWRO membranes in a bid to achieve higher flux [108]. However, this approach must be carefully dealt  with  for  SWRO  membranes  because  a  slight  decrease  in  crosslinking degree can result in a huge drop in salt rejection [95], rendering it impractical for SWRO taking into account the high requirement for salt rejection.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moving on, selecting an appropriate organic solvent in which MPD has higher solubility in as well as a lower organic solvent temperature can increase the amine to acid chloride ratio at the water/oil interface [102]. Consequently, the IP reaction rate and the degree of crosslinking will  be  greater,  thereby  forming  a  thicker  and  denser  selective  layer [102]. After the IP reaction is completed, the TFC membrane needs to undergo curing where it is annealed for stability [66] and to ensure the complete evaporation of the organic solvent [95]. An optimized curing process (in terms of temperature and duration) is needed to fabricate SWRO membranes with high permselectivity  because  it  controls  the crosslinking  degree  and  pore  size  of  the  selective  layer.  Other  researchers have also attempted to further fine-tune the permselectivity of SWRO  membranes  by  using  a  multi-stage  curing  process  [102]  or different mediums [98].

An optimized curing process (in terms of temperature and duration) is needed to fabricate SWRO membranes with high permselectivity  because  it  controls  the crosslinking  degree  and  pore  size  of  the  selective  layer.  Other  researchers have also attempted to further fine-tune the permselectivity of SWRO  membranes  by  using  a  multi-stage  curing  process  [102]  or different mediums [98].

texts.orig: Lastly,  although  most  SWRO  membranes  were  fabricated  using  a single  monomer (typically TMC) in the organic phase during IP, the feasibility of using mixed organic phase monomers in IP (Fig. 11A) have been explored, both in research papers [100,102] or patents [99,101]. Mixing monomers can accelerate the IP reaction by supplying more acyl chlorides at water/oil interface, thereby forming a thicker active layer with decreased water and salt permeability [102]. texts.orig: reaction between MPD and mixed organic phase monomers (Fig. 11B -C). The presence of the linear structure decreases the crosslinking degree thereby allowing the PA layer to permeate more water [102]. It should be noted that the ratio between the monomers must be optimized carefully because surface roughness, crosslinking degree and chain segment relaxation property change with a slight deviation in the ratio of the monomers [100,101]. The enhanced water flux is attributable to a much higher water diffusion across the membrane owing to a looser active layer. The salt permeability B increases as well, and so, mathematically, A/B increases,  resulting  in  increased  water  permeability at a constant salt rejection.

texts.orig: It  is  evident  that  the  PA  layer  of  SWRO  membranes  needs  to  be sufficiently crosslinked in order to attain > 99% NaCl rejection under high salinity conditions. To increase the crosslinking degree of the PA layer of SWRO membranes, it is essential to: i) increase the extent of IP reaction, ii) increase the IP reaction rate and iii) densify the nascent PA layer right after IP. In the first strategy, a combination of methods are typically adopted, such as:

In the first strategy, a combination of methods are typically adopted, such as:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Optimal reaction stoichiometry: MPD to TMC weight ratio of ~8 -30 (typically 2 -4 wt% MPD, 0.1 -0.3 wt% TMC) [71,105,107].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the second strategy, it is essential to manipulate the aqueous phase pH (typically  ~9 -10)  using  additives  such  as  TEA-CSA  salt  [95,100, 109]. It is well known that TEA-CSA salt can accelerate the IP reaction rate by consuming the reaction byproduct (HCl). Lastly, it is essential to finetune the curing process to densify and anneal the nascent PA layer so as to impart stability to the polymer chains. It has been suggested that curing at 60 -100 ◦ C with a duration of 5 -10 min is optimal for the formation of a sufficiently crosslinked PA in SWRO membrane fabrication [83,101,105].
texts.text: In the second strategy, it is essential to manipulate the aqueous phase pH (typically  ~9 -10)  using  additives  such  as  TEA-CSA  salt  [95,100, 109]. It is well known that TEA-CSA salt can accelerate the IP reaction rate by consuming the reaction byproduct (HCl). Lastly, it is essential to finetune the curing process to densify and anneal the nascent PA layer so as to impart stability to the polymer chains. It has been suggested that curing at 60 -100 ◦ C with a duration of 5 -10 min is optimal for the formation of a sufficiently crosslinked PA in SWRO membrane fabrication [83,101,105].

texts.orig: 3.2.2. Incorporation of additives and chemical compounds
texts.text: 3.2.2. Incorporation of additives and chemical compounds

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The incorporation of additives and chemicals during IP is another widely adopted approach for optimizing SWRO membrane separation performance. The major type of additives together with the reported performances in SWRO are summarized in Table 3.
texts.text: The incorporation of additives and chemicals during IP is another widely adopted approach for optimizing SWRO membrane separation performance. The major type of additives together with the reported performances in SWRO are summarized in Table 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  presence  of  additives  or  chemicals  typically  affects  the  IP mechanism in complicated ways because of its ability to interact with the monomers, as well as to induce physical and chemical changes to the PA matrix. In general, additives must be homogeneously dispersed prior to IP to harness the full benefits. For example, additives that are not dispersed properly can lead to agglomeration and thus defects in the PA layer, which consequently results in deterioration of separation performance [114]. Mixed additives can also be used to obtain synergistic benefits, but a comprehensive optimization is needed to obtain the best performance [119]. Overall, the additives in Table 3 can be classified into four main purposes:
texts.text: The  presence  of  additives  or  chemicals  typically  affects  the  IP mechanism in complicated ways because of its ability to interact with the monomers, as well as to induce physical and chemical changes to the PA matrix. In general, additives must be homogeneously dispersed prior to IP to harness the full benefits. For example, additives that are not dispersed properly can lead to agglomeration and thus defects in the PA layer, which consequently results in deterioration of separation performance [114]. Mixed additives can also be used to obtain synergistic benefits, but a comprehensive optimization is needed to obtain the best performance [119]. Overall, the additives in Table 3 can be classified into four main purposes:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Create free volumes in the PA layer. The incorporation of additives such as o -aminobenzoic acid -triethylamine ( o -ABA-TEA) salt [71] and dissolved aluminum ion species [107] is postulated to create more free volumes in the dense selective layer for water to diffuse through thereby leading to 62% and 34% enhanced flux of SWRO membranes, respectively.  Furthermore,  the  intrinsic  negative  charge  of  the o -ABA-TEA additive reinforces the negative charge on the PA layer, enabling the TFC membrane to attain both high permeability (2.59 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 ) and selectivity (99.4% NaCl rejection) in SWRO [71].
texts.text: · Create free volumes in the PA layer. The incorporation of additives such as o -aminobenzoic acid -triethylamine ( o -ABA-TEA) salt [71] and dissolved aluminum ion species [107] is postulated to create more free volumes in the dense selective layer for water to diffuse through thereby leading to 62% and 34% enhanced flux of SWRO membranes, respectively.  Furthermore,  the  intrinsic  negative  charge  of  the o -ABA-TEA additive reinforces the negative charge on the PA layer, enabling the TFC membrane to attain both high permeability (2.59 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 ) and selectivity (99.4% NaCl rejection) in SWRO [71].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Enhance roughness of the PA film. For SWRO membranes, the methodology of fabricating rougher PA films to enhance the flux has shown success by using an additional IP reaction involving MPD dissolved in alcohols [112]. Since the concentration of the non-reacted acyl chloride  on  the  nascent  PA  layer  is  spatially  different,  the  degree  of polymerization induced by the additional amine groups leads to PA films with different heights, thereby forming a three-dimensional (3D) reticular structure with a very rough surface. Although this method made the PA layer thicker (350 -400 nm), the patent disclosed that the increase in roughness helped to offset it such that a flux enhancement of 22% is still obtained. Another report was to incorporate fluorinated mono glycidyl ether compounds in the aqueous phase for IP [115]. The fluorine compounds lowered the reactivity of the amine monomer which generated a huge difference in the polymerization rate [115]. Because of the spatial difference in PA formation, a very rough PA layer can be formed.
texts.text: · Enhance roughness of the PA film. For SWRO membranes, the methodology of fabricating rougher PA films to enhance the flux has shown success by using an additional IP reaction involving MPD dissolved in alcohols [112]. Since the concentration of the non-reacted acyl chloride  on  the  nascent  PA  layer  is  spatially  different,  the  degree  of polymerization induced by the additional amine groups leads to PA films with different heights, thereby forming a three-dimensional (3D) reticular structure with a very rough surface. Although this method made the PA layer thicker (350 -400 nm), the patent disclosed that the increase in roughness helped to offset it such that a flux enhancement of 22% is still obtained. Another report was to incorporate fluorinated mono glycidyl ether compounds in the aqueous phase for IP [115]. The fluorine compounds lowered the reactivity of the amine monomer which generated a huge difference in the polymerization rate [115]. Because of the spatial difference in PA formation, a very rough PA layer can be formed.

On the other hand, the enhancement of surface charge led to a higher salt  rejection  because  the  solutes  are  more  electrostatically  repelled. Using the same concept, the incorporation of epoxy/fluorine-containing additives had also shown similar successes [116]. More importantly, the patent also disclosed that the ratio between the epoxy compounds to the amine monomer should be 1:3 in order to increase the chain length of the material forming the passivation layer. This will then expose the fluorine/epoxy-rich segments at the outermost surface so as to maximize charge repulsion effect for optimal solute rejection.

Using the same concept, the incorporation of epoxy/fluorine-containing additives had also shown similar successes [116]. More importantly, the patent also disclosed that the ratio between the epoxy compounds to the amine monomer should be 1:3 in order to increase the chain length of the material forming the passivation layer. This will then expose the fluorine/epoxy-rich segments at the outermost surface so as to maximize charge repulsion effect for optimal solute rejection.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Lower crosslinking degree of the PA layer. Manifold studies have outlined the possibility of fabricating SWRO membranes with higher permselectivity by making the selective layer slightly looser. To achieve this, some of the approaches include the incorporation of β -diketonate salts [113], metal chelate additives [107,110,117] and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) [110] in the aqueous phase or inclusion complexes with metal chelates [118] in  the organic phase. The salt rejection  can  be maintained either by a dilution effect or an enhancement of charge to repel the solutes. For example, β -diketonate salts can dissociate to form cations and anions thereby interfering with the covalent bonds in the PA matrix, leading to the formation of ionic crosslinks between the hydrolyzed acid chloride and terminal amine groups [113]. The presence of these crosslinks can lead to a slight expansion of the 3D PA matrix ( i.e. a less 'tight ' PA layer) which enhanced the SWRO membrane flux by 56%. Simultaneously, the selectivity of the SWRO membrane can be preserved because  the  ionic  crosslinks  enhanced  the  capacity  for  repelling  the charged  solutes  (Na + ,  Cl )  more  effectively.  The  incorporation  of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) had also shown success in enhancing the flux of SWRO membranes [110]. Being a co-solvent, DMSO is known to increase the miscibility of the two phases in IP which can lead to a less crosslinked PA matrix [108]. The presence of dissolved alkaline metal ions from metal chelate additives can affect the mechanisms of IP [110, 118]. For example, it was reported that the dissolved metal additives can precipitate  and  give  off  heat  at  the  water/oil  interface.  The  heat generated can affect the packing of nascent polymer chains leading to an altered PA matrix structure with a greater free volume [107]. Because of the higher free volume, the PA layer is more permeable to water leading to 31% flux enhancement. The patent also revealed that the size of metal
