texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.3. Incorporation of filler materials
texts.text: 3.2.3. Incorporation of filler materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent  studies  using  novel  nanomaterials  for  desalination  membranes have shown the potential for extremely high water permeability surpassing that of commercial state-of-the-art membranes. As shown in Table 4, a myriad of filler materials has been explored for fabrication of SWRO membranes. The purpose is to enhance the membrane ' s permselectivity,  boron  rejection,  chlorine  resistance  or  reduce  the  fouling propensity. It is well acknowledged that the incorporation of filler materials in the PA layer can lead to defect formation (especially when they agglomerate  [120]),  thereby  compromising  membrane  selectivity. Hence, during the fabrication, filler materials must be reasonably stable and  easily  processable  to  minimize  defect  formation.  One  common approach is to disperse the nanomaterials ( e.g. using ultrasonication) [12] into small particle sizes ( < 1000 nm) and uniformly distribute them across the PA layer [121]. Smaller nanoparticles are preferred because of the higher specific surface exposure which will increase their solubility or dispersity. For example, an SWRO membrane patent outlined that silver filler particles should be ~2 -10 nm in size and occupy up to only  1 -5% of the PA film thickness [122] to prevent filler exposure through the selective layer.
texts.text: Recent  studies  using  novel  nanomaterials  for  desalination  membranes have shown the potential for extremely high water permeability surpassing that of commercial state-of-the-art membranes. As shown in Table 4, a myriad of filler materials has been explored for fabrication of SWRO membranes. The purpose is to enhance the membrane ' s permselectivity,  boron  rejection,  chlorine  resistance  or  reduce  the  fouling propensity. It is well acknowledged that the incorporation of filler materials in the PA layer can lead to defect formation (especially when they agglomerate  [120]),  thereby  compromising  membrane  selectivity. Hence, during the fabrication, filler materials must be reasonably stable and  easily  processable  to  minimize  defect  formation.  One  common approach is to disperse the nanomaterials ( e.g. using ultrasonication) [12] into small particle sizes ( < 1000 nm) and uniformly distribute them across the PA layer [121]. Smaller nanoparticles are preferred because of the higher specific surface exposure which will increase their solubility or dispersity. For example, an SWRO membrane patent outlined that silver filler particles should be ~2 -10 nm in size and occupy up to only  1 -5% of the PA film thickness [122] to prevent filler exposure through the selective layer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is noteworthy to mention that a combination of filler materials can be incorporated in the selective layer, forming hybrid thin film nanocomposite  SWRO  (TFN-SWRO)  membranes  [107].  However,  careful optimization is necessary to prevent particle agglomeration and defect formation so as to create the synergistic effect desired. The filler materials in Table 4 can be generally categorized into the following types:
texts.text: It is noteworthy to mention that a combination of filler materials can be incorporated in the selective layer, forming hybrid thin film nanocomposite  SWRO  (TFN-SWRO)  membranes  [107].  However,  careful optimization is necessary to prevent particle agglomeration and defect formation so as to create the synergistic effect desired. The filler materials in Table 4 can be generally categorized into the following types:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Zeolites. There  has  been  considerable  research  on  TFN-SWRO membranes incorporating zeolite compounds in the selective layer. Zeolites  are  a  class  of  aluminosilicate  mineral  with  microstructures comprising pores of size ~0.3 -0.8 nm which are between the diameters of hydrated salt ions ( e.g. Na + : 0.72 nm) and water molecules (0.27 nm) (Fig.  12A)  [120].  Based  on  the  size  exclusion  effect,  zeolites  should allow the preferential permeation of water over salt ions. In the synthesis  of  SWRO  membranes,  zeolites  can  be  incorporated  into  the organic phase [96,107] or aqueous phase [107,121] in IP. Lind et al. [96]  reported  a  12 -16%  water  flux  enhancement  at  similar  salt  rejections, which they attributed to both molecular sieving and defects formation  in  the  TFN-SWRO  membranes  despite  an  extremely  low zeolite concentration used (0.2 wt%). Also, the enhanced flux of SWRO membranes incorporated with zeolites can be found to arise from other factors such as lower crosslinking degree [96], enhanced hydrophilicity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: must be in the range of 1.5 -3.5 Å to obtain high NaCl rejection (99.7%) under SWRO conditions [87]. Also, the surface treated zeolites should have  uniform  sizes  in  the  range  of  50 -200  nm  in  diameter  and  the concentration should be < 1 wt% in the MPD phase to achieve better dispersion,  which  ultimately  minimized  defect  occurrence  in  the 150 -200 nm thick PA layer. Similarly, iron oxyhydroxide coated zeolites, with nanoporous core surrounded by iron oxyhydroxide, should have sizes between 50 and 400 nm in diameter [123]. To achieve the high salt rejection of 99.3% in SWRO, the pore size of the core must be ~0.3 -1.2 nm to reject NaCl ions. More importantly, coated zeolites also increased the surface charge amount and hydrophilicity of the SWRO membrane, thereby improving the water permeability.

Also, the surface treated zeolites should have  uniform  sizes  in  the  range  of  50 -200  nm  in  diameter  and  the concentration should be < 1 wt% in the MPD phase to achieve better dispersion,  which  ultimately  minimized  defect  occurrence  in  the 150 -200 nm thick PA layer. Similarly, iron oxyhydroxide coated zeolites, with nanoporous core surrounded by iron oxyhydroxide, should have sizes between 50 and 400 nm in diameter [123]. To achieve the high salt rejection of 99.3% in SWRO, the pore size of the core must be ~0.3 -1.2 nm to reject NaCl ions. More importantly, coated zeolites also increased the surface charge amount and hydrophilicity of the SWRO membrane, thereby improving the water permeability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Carbon-based materials. Inorganic  filler  materials  such  as  graphene oxide (GO) [124], graphite [125] and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been explored in the fabrication of SWRO membranes. GO is a graphene derivative functionalized with oxygen-abundant groups ( e.g. hydroxyl,  carboxyl  and  ether  groups),  whereas  CNTs  are  cylindrical tubes (up to micrometers in length) conceptually rolled up from graphene sheets [120]. The interlayer spacing between GO nanosheets and the inner diameter of CNTs can serve as nanochannels for water transport  (Fig.  12B  and  C)  [134].  These  nanochannels  of  carbon-based nanomaterials have smooth hydrophobic surface on non-oxidized regions, which possess high slip length and low friction transport for water molecules [134], rendering them as potential candidates in the development of next generation membranes.
texts.text: · Carbon-based materials. Inorganic  filler  materials  such  as  graphene oxide (GO) [124], graphite [125] and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been explored in the fabrication of SWRO membranes. GO is a graphene derivative functionalized with oxygen-abundant groups ( e.g. hydroxyl,  carboxyl  and  ether  groups),  whereas  CNTs  are  cylindrical tubes (up to micrometers in length) conceptually rolled up from graphene sheets [120]. The interlayer spacing between GO nanosheets and the inner diameter of CNTs can serve as nanochannels for water transport  (Fig.  12B  and  C)  [134].  These  nanochannels  of  carbon-based nanomaterials have smooth hydrophobic surface on non-oxidized regions, which possess high slip length and low friction transport for water molecules [134], rendering them as potential candidates in the development of next generation membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, CNTs can control water transport by nanoconfinement and electronic effects [135]. Kurth et al. [107] reported that the use of CNTs with smaller diameters (1 -2 nm) enhance the water flux of SWRO membranes by 29% without compromising salt rejection because the smaller  nanochannels  of  CNTs  can  sieve  out  hydrated  ions  while allowing water to permeate through readily. However, to achieve this, CNTs bundles must be broken up and the length of CNTs should be shortened to ~0.5 -2 μ m. The patent also revealed the incorporation of zwitterionic surfactants to disperse and align the CNTs within the PA layer.  Such  post-synthetic  CNT  alignment  can  be  carried  out  in  two ways: (1) vertical alignment (perpendicular to the superficial PA surface) or (2) horizontal alignment (on the same plane as the membrane surface). The former is technically challenging and can be achieved to a certain extent with external aids such as magnetic catalysts or surfactants. The latter is more feasible and can be easily realized by applying shear force to the coating solution [134].
texts.text: For example, CNTs can control water transport by nanoconfinement and electronic effects [135]. Kurth et al. [107] reported that the use of CNTs with smaller diameters (1 -2 nm) enhance the water flux of SWRO membranes by 29% without compromising salt rejection because the smaller  nanochannels  of  CNTs  can  sieve  out  hydrated  ions  while allowing water to permeate through readily. However, to achieve this, CNTs bundles must be broken up and the length of CNTs should be shortened to ~0.5 -2 μ m. The patent also revealed the incorporation of zwitterionic surfactants to disperse and align the CNTs within the PA layer.  Such  post-synthetic  CNT  alignment  can  be  carried  out  in  two ways: (1) vertical alignment (perpendicular to the superficial PA surface) or (2) horizontal alignment (on the same plane as the membrane surface). The former is technically challenging and can be achieved to a certain extent with external aids such as magnetic catalysts or surfactants. The latter is more feasible and can be easily realized by applying shear force to the coating solution [134].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In contrast to GO and CNTs, graphite nanoparticles were added to tune the physicochemical properties of the PA layer. Zhang et al. [125] attributed  the  47%  flux  enhancement  of  SWRO  membranes  to  the reduced crosslinking degree, and increased hydrophilicity induced by graphite nanoparticles in the PA layer. It was highlighted that the concentration of graphite in organic phase cannot exceed 0.006 wt% or else the  water  pathways  on  the  membrane  substrate  can  be  blocked  by graphite. In addition, high concentrations of graphite can lead to aggregation and formation of large leaf-like structures on the PA, thereby decreasing the specific surface area of the TFN membrane and leading to declined flux.
texts.text: In contrast to GO and CNTs, graphite nanoparticles were added to tune the physicochemical properties of the PA layer. Zhang et al. [125] attributed  the  47%  flux  enhancement  of  SWRO  membranes  to  the reduced crosslinking degree, and increased hydrophilicity induced by graphite nanoparticles in the PA layer. It was highlighted that the concentration of graphite in organic phase cannot exceed 0.006 wt% or else the  water  pathways  on  the  membrane  substrate  can  be  blocked  by graphite. In addition, high concentrations of graphite can lead to aggregation and formation of large leaf-like structures on the PA, thereby decreasing the specific surface area of the TFN membrane and leading to declined flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Silica nanomaterials. The advantages of silica nanomaterials such as  high  specific  surface  area  (~100 -250 m 2 /g) [129], tunable nanostructures,  mesopores  with  high  uniformity  and  hydrophilicity  have made it a promising material in the field of membrane fabrication. The use of mesoporous silica has been explored in the fabrication of SWRO membranes by incorporating them in the aqueous phase of IP [128]. It was postulated that water transport across the PA layer is accelerated because of the diffusion of water molecules through the pores of mesoporous  silica,  which  function  as  a  transport  channel  of  low  water resistance. Wang et al. [128] postulated that the aminophenyl groups on the  PA  can  form  covalent  bonds  with  the  mesoporous  silica,  which impart greater stability to the PA layer to embed the SWRO membrane with higher salt rejection. It was reported that the size of the mesoporous silica had particle sizes of ~40 nm with a pore (channel) size of 2 -3 nm.
texts.text: · Silica nanomaterials. The advantages of silica nanomaterials such as  high  specific  surface  area  (~100 -250 m 2 /g) [129], tunable nanostructures,  mesopores  with  high  uniformity  and  hydrophilicity  have made it a promising material in the field of membrane fabrication. The use of mesoporous silica has been explored in the fabrication of SWRO membranes by incorporating them in the aqueous phase of IP [128]. It was postulated that water transport across the PA layer is accelerated because of the diffusion of water molecules through the pores of mesoporous  silica,  which  function  as  a  transport  channel  of  low  water resistance. Wang et al. [128] postulated that the aminophenyl groups on the  PA  can  form  covalent  bonds  with  the  mesoporous  silica,  which impart greater stability to the PA layer to embed the SWRO membrane with higher salt rejection. It was reported that the size of the mesoporous silica had particle sizes of ~40 nm with a pore (channel) size of 2 -3 nm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from the creation of water channels, it was reported that fumed silica nanoparticles in the organic phase can inhibit the diffusion of MPD into the organic phase during IP [129]. Thus, MPD will accumulate at the  interface  leading  to  increased  local  MPD  concentration.  Consequently, it results in a denser and thicker PA layer which explains the higher salt rejection, but slightly lower permeability.
texts.text: Apart from the creation of water channels, it was reported that fumed silica nanoparticles in the organic phase can inhibit the diffusion of MPD into the organic phase during IP [129]. Thus, MPD will accumulate at the  interface  leading  to  increased  local  MPD  concentration.  Consequently, it results in a denser and thicker PA layer which explains the higher salt rejection, but slightly lower permeability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Metal-organic  frameworks  (MOFs). The  success  of  TFN-SWRO membranes  incorporated  with  MOFs  includes  [Cu3(TMA)2(H2O)3]n complex  (commonly  known  as  HKUST-1)  [107],  UiO-66  [109]  or UiO-66-NH2  nanoparticles pre-deposited by spraying [130]. Theoretically, the inherently porous structure of MOFs can act as low resistance water channels to accelerate water transport through the PA layer. For example, HKUST-1 comprises an intersecting 3D structure with channel sizes of ~1 nm [136] while UiO-66 (50 -100 nm in size to minimize defect formation) possesses a channel diameter ~6.1 Å (Fig. 12D and E) and  a  high  specific  surface  area  of  834 -1122 m 2 /g  [109,130].  Both MOFs have shown success in the fabrication of SWRO membranes with enhanced flux of ~19 -43%. For UiO-66, researchers have also attributed  the  flux  enhancement  to  the  higher  hydrophilicity  and  PA  free volumes ( i.e., decreased crosslinking degree) [109,130]. In addition, the charge-exclusion effect induced by UiO-66 helps to maintain the salt rejection [109]. More importantly, the concentration of UiO-66 cannot exceed  0.05  w/v%  because  this  will  lead  to  a  thicker  active  layer (thereby decreasing permeability) and that the aggregation of UiO-66 will  lead  to  formation  of  defects  (thereby  decreasing  selectivity).  It should be noted that whilst most publications or patents attributed the permselectivity improvement to the incorporation of filler materials in the PA layer, there is no clear characterization evidence ( e.g. by transmission electron microscopy (TEM)) of the presence of filler materials embedded within the  PA  layer.  Hence,  the  mechanisms  surrounding performance improvements of TFN membranes remains equivocal at the current stage.
texts.text: · Metal-organic  frameworks  (MOFs). The  success  of  TFN-SWRO membranes  incorporated  with  MOFs  includes  [Cu3(TMA)2(H2O)3]n complex  (commonly  known  as  HKUST-1)  [107],  UiO-66  [109]  or UiO-66-NH2  nanoparticles pre-deposited by spraying [130]. Theoretically, the inherently porous structure of MOFs can act as low resistance water channels to accelerate water transport through the PA layer. For example, HKUST-1 comprises an intersecting 3D structure with channel sizes of ~1 nm [136] while UiO-66 (50 -100 nm in size to minimize defect formation) possesses a channel diameter ~6.1 Å (Fig. 12D and E) and  a  high  specific  surface  area  of  834 -1122 m 2 /g  [109,130].  Both MOFs have shown success in the fabrication of SWRO membranes with enhanced flux of ~19 -43%. For UiO-66, researchers have also attributed  the  flux  enhancement  to  the  higher  hydrophilicity  and  PA  free volumes ( i.e., decreased crosslinking degree) [109,130]. In addition, the charge-exclusion effect induced by UiO-66 helps to maintain the salt rejection [109]. More importantly, the concentration of UiO-66 cannot exceed  0.05  w/v%  because  this  will  lead  to  a  thicker  active  layer (thereby decreasing permeability) and that the aggregation of UiO-66 will  lead  to  formation  of  defects  (thereby  decreasing  selectivity).  It should be noted that whilst most publications or patents attributed the permselectivity improvement to the incorporation of filler materials in the PA layer, there is no clear characterization evidence ( e.g. by transmission electron microscopy (TEM)) of the presence of filler materials embedded within the  PA  layer.  Hence,  the  mechanisms  surrounding performance improvements of TFN membranes remains equivocal at the current stage.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.4. Biomimetic SWRO membranes
texts.text: 3.2.4. Biomimetic SWRO membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The outstanding water transport properties of biological membranes have led to the interest in the development of biomimetic membranes for desalination. The fabrication of biomimetic membranes are based on the incorporation of biological water channels ( i.e., aquaporin (AQP)) or synthetic water nanochannels within lipid bilayers [137] or amphiphilic block copolymers [138]. For RO membranes, specially designed water channels could lead to a shift in how water is transported through the membrane  via fast single-file transport through water channels embedded within the PA selective layer. Thus, biomimetic membranes incorporating AQPs are projected to have both high permeability and selectivity  theoretically.  Recently,  Li  et  al.  [82]  reported  the  first AQP-based  biomimetic  membrane  for  SWRO  desalination  (refer  to Table  5).  The  AQPs  were  embedded  into  proteoliposomes  ( i.e., protein-containing lipid vesicles) (Fig. 13A) and subsequently incorporated  into  the  MPD  aqueous  phase,  with  the  rest  of  the  fabrication approach similar to that of conventional IP. The AQP-based biomimetic SWRO membrane showed 100% flux enhancement as compared to the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ultrafast transport of liquids in biological cellular membranes has led to an interest in creating biomimetic membranes using nanostructures.  In  another  recent  attempt,  biomimetic  SWRO  membranes embedded with polymersomes was first reported by Qi and coworkers [139].  The  polymersomes  artificial  vesicles  with  spherical  nanostructures were formed by the self-assembly of an amphipathic tri-block copolymer.  The  polymersomes  were  dissolved  in  the  MPD  aqueous phase and immobilized into the PA layer via conventional IP (Fig. 13B). It was postulated that the high permeability of the polymersomes-based SWRO membrane was attributed to the presence of hollow polymersomes  which  possessed  a  higher  void  fraction  in  the  selective  layer induced by the large globular features of the polymersomes [139]. On the other hand, salt rejection was maintained because of the polymersomes bilayer ' s impermeability to NaCl and that the intrinsic PA layer remained still highly crosslinked despite voids induced by the polymersomes.
texts.text: The ultrafast transport of liquids in biological cellular membranes has led to an interest in creating biomimetic membranes using nanostructures.  In  another  recent  attempt,  biomimetic  SWRO  membranes embedded with polymersomes was first reported by Qi and coworkers [139].  The  polymersomes  artificial  vesicles  with  spherical  nanostructures were formed by the self-assembly of an amphipathic tri-block copolymer.  The  polymersomes  were  dissolved  in  the  MPD  aqueous phase and immobilized into the PA layer via conventional IP (Fig. 13B). It was postulated that the high permeability of the polymersomes-based SWRO membrane was attributed to the presence of hollow polymersomes  which  possessed  a  higher  void  fraction  in  the  selective  layer induced by the large globular features of the polymersomes [139]. On the other hand, salt rejection was maintained because of the polymersomes bilayer ' s impermeability to NaCl and that the intrinsic PA layer remained still highly crosslinked despite voids induced by the polymersomes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Of all the next-generation RO membranes currently in development, it was argued that biomimetic membranes hold the greatest promise to fabricate  membranes  with  ultrahigh  selectivity,  and  are  projected  to yield unprecedented benefits in SWRO [142]. For example, modeling results from Werber et al. [141] predicted that a defect-free biomimetic membrane containing 3% aquaporin (AQP)-Z can technically achieve > 99.999% salt rejection in standard SWRO conditions. It is of no surprise that a near perfect rejection can be obtained because AQP-Z can almost reject all solutes theoretically, and thus salt permeability will be almost  zero.  However,  the  permselectivity  of  current  biomimetic membranes  are  far  below  theoretical  predictions,  especially  the  salt rejections reported remain lower than expected [143]. It is postulated that the discrepancy between experimental and modeling results was due to the presence of defects in the selective layer. To overcome this problem,  Werber  et  al.  [141]  proposed  to  fabricate  the  biomimetic active layer on top of a PA selective layer ( e.g. RO membranes) (Fig. 13C) instead of on the porous support membrane commonly adopted by researchers  (Fig.  13D).  Thus,  the  defects  in  the  biomimetic  layer  will expose the PA selective layer instead of the support layer, thereby preserving the high selectivity of the biomimetic composite membrane even if  there  are  inevitable  amounts  of  defects  on  the  biomimetic  layer. Because AQP-Z can almost fully reject all solutes including neutral solutes like boron, it is argued that biomimetic membranes incorporating AQP-Z hold the highest potential to fabricate SWRO membranes with ultrahigh salt and boron rejections (99.9%) which are highly desired in seawater desalination. However, similar to the TFN membranes, there has been no TEM confirmation that the AQPs are indeed embedded in the PA selective layer. Hence, the mechanisms surrounding the performance  improvement  of  biomimetic  membranes  are  still  debatable. Furthermore, the interfacial defects between AQPs and the PA matrix have not been thoroughly studied at the current stage. Hence, it is recommended that future works examine the interfacial morphology and chemistry  between  AQPs  and  the  PA  matrix  to  elucidate  the  fundamental mechanisms behind the performance enhancements of AQP-based biomimetic membranes.

texts.text: 3.2.5. Surface coatings and modifications of SWRO membranes

texts.orig: A summary of surface coatings and modification works for SWRO membranes is shown in Table 6, together with their overall membrane performances. Surface coating is typically adopted to chemically modify the surface properties of the PA layer.

texts.text: between unreacted amines and acid chlorides on the PA layer, leading to a tighter PA layer [86]. Heating is usually required after the coating to facilitate additional crosslinking between the modifier and PA. Surface coating  related  to  enhancing  boron  rejection  will  be  elaborated  in greater detail in Section 3.3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Increase the stability of the PA layer. Surface coating can be a facile method to impart greater stability to the PA selective layer. For example,  it  is  well-known  that  the  curing  process  can  lead  to  the shrinkage of PA pores which can reduce the flux of RO membranes. SWRO membrane patents have outlined the feasibility of using surface coating to protect the PA pores and matrix from shrinkage during curing, thereby maintaining the salt rejection without sacrificing the membrane permeability [149,150]. In addition, another SWRO patent reported that the increased stability of the PA layer imparted by surface coating can lead to a higher chlorine resistance of the SWRO membrane [145].
texts.text: · Increase the stability of the PA layer. Surface coating can be a facile method to impart greater stability to the PA selective layer. For example,  it  is  well-known  that  the  curing  process  can  lead  to  the shrinkage of PA pores which can reduce the flux of RO membranes. SWRO membrane patents have outlined the feasibility of using surface coating to protect the PA pores and matrix from shrinkage during curing, thereby maintaining the salt rejection without sacrificing the membrane permeability [149,150]. In addition, another SWRO patent reported that the increased stability of the PA layer imparted by surface coating can lead to a higher chlorine resistance of the SWRO membrane [145].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Impart anti-fouling, anti-adhesion and anti-microbial properties. The use of surface coatings to enhance anti-fouling properties of TFC membranes  has  been  widely  adopted  by  commercial  RO  membrane manufacturers.  For  instance,  neutral  hydrophilic  coatings  such  as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is often applied to enhance anti-fouling properties of SWRO membranes because of its ability to neutralize surface charge, reduce surface roughness and improve membrane surface hydrophilicity. Many SWRO membrane patents have explored the feasibility  of  various  hydrophilic  coatings  such  as  metal  oxides  [145], fluorine  groups  [147,148],  amphoteric  ionic  compounds  [146]  and glycerin [150] to increase the hydrophilicity of the PA layer in a bid to decrease fouling propensity. Thus, coated membranes see better flux recovery  after  cleaning  as  compared  to  uncoated  membrane  [149]. Layer-by-layer deposition of PA films can also lead to the formation of a smooth, conformal and hydrophilic selective layer which can decrease SWRO  membrane  fouling  propensity  [144].  Anti-fouling  strategies related to surface coatings will be elaborated in greater detail in Section 3.4.
texts.text: · Impart anti-fouling, anti-adhesion and anti-microbial properties. The use of surface coatings to enhance anti-fouling properties of TFC membranes  has  been  widely  adopted  by  commercial  RO  membrane manufacturers.  For  instance,  neutral  hydrophilic  coatings  such  as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is often applied to enhance anti-fouling properties of SWRO membranes because of its ability to neutralize surface charge, reduce surface roughness and improve membrane surface hydrophilicity. Many SWRO membrane patents have explored the feasibility  of  various  hydrophilic  coatings  such  as  metal  oxides  [145], fluorine  groups  [147,148],  amphoteric  ionic  compounds  [146]  and glycerin [150] to increase the hydrophilicity of the PA layer in a bid to decrease fouling propensity. Thus, coated membranes see better flux recovery  after  cleaning  as  compared  to  uncoated  membrane  [149]. Layer-by-layer deposition of PA films can also lead to the formation of a smooth, conformal and hydrophilic selective layer which can decrease SWRO  membrane  fouling  propensity  [144].  Anti-fouling  strategies related to surface coatings will be elaborated in greater detail in Section 3.4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the wide range of benefits brought by surface coatings and modifications, it is worth noting that the membrane permeability usually decreases due to the additional hydraulic resistance imposed by the coating layer. Hence, surface coatings must be well designed to maximize the benefits reaped and minimize the compromises imposed. If the coating  leads  to  a  decrease  in  membrane  permeability,  the  benefits gained from the anti-fouling properties must justify the loss in initial water flux to add value [149].

Hence, surface coatings must be well designed to maximize the benefits reaped and minimize the compromises imposed. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Enhancing boron rejection strategies
texts.text: 3.3. Enhancing boron rejection strategies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, many research works have attempted to enhance the boron rejection of SWRO membranes (refer to Table 7) by modifying the PA layer chemistry and physical structure. In general, the works can be classified into four main categories.
texts.text: In recent years, many research works have attempted to enhance the boron rejection of SWRO membranes (refer to Table 7) by modifying the PA layer chemistry and physical structure. In general, the works can be classified into four main categories.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Introduce 'plugging ' effect on the PA layer. According to literature, the PA layer of FT-30 type SWRO membrane contains mesoporous defects  in  dry  state  which  compromises its ability to remove small uncharged molecules like boric acid [40]. Hence, many researchers have proposed the 'plugging ' of the mesoporous pores method to block boron permeation across the PA layer (Fig. 14A). For example, it was shown that SWRO membranes with enhanced boron rejection can be fabricated by  the  in-situ  sorption  of  amine  molecules  on  the  PA  layer  (surface modification) [46] or the incorporation of silica fillers in IP [129]. Both approaches are based on the principle of blocking the mesoporous defects on the PA layer thereby decreasing boron permeation.
texts.text: Introduce 'plugging ' effect on the PA layer. According to literature, the PA layer of FT-30 type SWRO membrane contains mesoporous defects  in  dry  state  which  compromises its ability to remove small uncharged molecules like boric acid [40]. Hence, many researchers have proposed the 'plugging ' of the mesoporous pores method to block boron permeation across the PA layer (Fig. 14A). For example, it was shown that SWRO membranes with enhanced boron rejection can be fabricated by  the  in-situ  sorption  of  amine  molecules  on  the  PA  layer  (surface modification) [46] or the incorporation of silica fillers in IP [129]. Both approaches are based on the principle of blocking the mesoporous defects on the PA layer thereby decreasing boron permeation.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Enhance the electrostatic and charge repulsion effect. It is well established  that  both  size  exclusion  and  charge  repulsion  effects  can enhance the boron rejection of SWRO membranes [95]. As an example, the  incorporation  of  sulfonyl  molecular  plugs  onto  the  PA  layer  can introduce  electrostatic  repulsion  towards  boric  acid  molecules  [43]. Consequently, this leads to enhanced boron rejection because boric acid molecules are more readily repelled by the PA layer.
texts.text: Enhance the electrostatic and charge repulsion effect. It is well established  that  both  size  exclusion  and  charge  repulsion  effects  can enhance the boron rejection of SWRO membranes [95]. As an example, the  incorporation  of  sulfonyl  molecular  plugs  onto  the  PA  layer  can introduce  electrostatic  repulsion  towards  boric  acid  molecules  [43]. Consequently, this leads to enhanced boron rejection because boric acid molecules are more readily repelled by the PA layer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Introduce  functional  moieties  onto  the  PA  layer. SWRO membranes with enhanced boron rejection can be fabricated by introducing functional groups that have the ability to adsorb boron. For example, TFN  membranes  with  UiO-66  fillers  can  promote  boron  adsorption within the pores of UiO-66 [109] which leads to a pore narrowing effect thereby hindering boron permeation across the filler materials (Fig. 14B). The incorporation of sulfonate functional groups on the PA layer (Fig. 14C) can also induce charge-aggregate cavities that chelate boric  acid  [152]  thereby  hindering  the  latter ' s  passage  through  the SWRO membrane.
texts.text: · Introduce  functional  moieties  onto  the  PA  layer. SWRO membranes with enhanced boron rejection can be fabricated by introducing functional groups that have the ability to adsorb boron. For example, TFN  membranes  with  UiO-66  fillers  can  promote  boron  adsorption within the pores of UiO-66 [109] which leads to a pore narrowing effect thereby hindering boron permeation across the filler materials (Fig. 14B). The incorporation of sulfonate functional groups on the PA layer (Fig. 14C) can also induce charge-aggregate cavities that chelate boric  acid  [152]  thereby  hindering  the  latter ' s  passage  through  the SWRO membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Manifold investigations have also outlined the possibility of increasing the crosslinking degree of the PA layer to decrease boron permeation. To 'tighten ' the PA network, additional covalent bonding via  surface  coatings  with  oxazoline-based  compounds  [86]  or  the

The  boron  rejections  were  measured  at  neutral  pH unless otherwise stated (the arrow outlines the boron rejection improvement with  respect  to  the  control  membrane).  The  prefix  IP  and  SC  denotes  the approach of incorporating nanomaterials/additives in interfacial polymerization and surface coating respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: incorporation of compounds, such as polyisobutylene (PIB) [48], polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) [117] or iodine [151], in IP have reported success in enhancing boron rejection. The presence of these compounds were postulated to interpenetrate the PA layer (Fig. 14D), leading  to  more  entanglements  and  crosslinking  which  can  lead  to decrease in PA free volumes. In addition, the incorporation of PIB was reported to make the PA layer more hydrophobic which consequently decreased the hydrogen bonding accepting sites on the selective layer [48]. Hence, the binding propensity of boric acid to the selective layer via hydrogen bonding was greatly reduced hindering boron transport across the PA layer and leading to enhanced boron rejection.
texts.text: incorporation of compounds, such as polyisobutylene (PIB) [48], polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) [117] or iodine [151], in IP have reported success in enhancing boron rejection. The presence of these compounds were postulated to interpenetrate the PA layer (Fig. 14D), leading  to  more  entanglements  and  crosslinking  which  can  lead  to decrease in PA free volumes. In addition, the incorporation of PIB was reported to make the PA layer more hydrophobic which consequently decreased the hydrogen bonding accepting sites on the selective layer [48]. Hence, the binding propensity of boric acid to the selective layer via hydrogen bonding was greatly reduced hindering boron transport across the PA layer and leading to enhanced boron rejection.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Anti-fouling strategies
texts.text: 3.4. Anti-fouling strategies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed earlier in Section 2.6, TFC-SWRO membranes are prone to fouling because of their inherently hydrophobic surface properties. Therefore, many works (refer to Table 8) have attempted to tailor the physicochemical properties of the PA layer to minimize the adsorption of microorganisms, biomaterials and organic foulants on the membrane surface.
texts.text: As discussed earlier in Section 2.6, TFC-SWRO membranes are prone to fouling because of their inherently hydrophobic surface properties. Therefore, many works (refer to Table 8) have attempted to tailor the physicochemical properties of the PA layer to minimize the adsorption of microorganisms, biomaterials and organic foulants on the membrane surface.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, the works summarized in Table 8 can be classified into two main categories:
texts.text: In general, the works summarized in Table 8 can be classified into two main categories:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Reduce roughness of the PA layer. For fouling control, a reduction in the roughness will mean that it is less prone to trap foulants on the PA layer surface. In the fabrication of SWRO membranes, a smoother PA layer  can  be  formed  by  the incorporation of additives [71] or nanomaterials [56,107] in IP as well as the optimization of the support layer surface pore properties. For example, it was reported that the support layer with uniform pore sizes of 20 nm can reduce fouling propensity [80], presumably due to reduced surface roughness because of a more uniform migration of MPD monomers during IP. A smoother PA layer can also facilitate peeling of the foulant layer [56], thereby increasing flux recovery during membrane cleaning.
texts.text: · Reduce roughness of the PA layer. For fouling control, a reduction in the roughness will mean that it is less prone to trap foulants on the PA layer surface. In the fabrication of SWRO membranes, a smoother PA layer  can  be  formed  by  the incorporation of additives [71] or nanomaterials [56,107] in IP as well as the optimization of the support layer surface pore properties. For example, it was reported that the support layer with uniform pore sizes of 20 nm can reduce fouling propensity [80], presumably due to reduced surface roughness because of a more uniform migration of MPD monomers during IP. A smoother PA layer can also facilitate peeling of the foulant layer [56], thereby increasing flux recovery during membrane cleaning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Increase the hydrophilicity and surface charge of the PA layer. A more  hydrophilic  PA  layer  typically  have  higher  fouling  resistance because of lower foulant adsorption [12,120]. There has been manifold anti-fouling strategies explored based on this concept, namely, incorporating additives [71,111,155] and nanomaterials [107,127] or surface  coating  with  hydrophilic  compounds.  In  the  first  approach,  the inability of certain nanomaterials ( e.g. multiwalled CNTs) to form bonds with foulants can lead to decreased fouling propensity because hydrophobic  foulants  cannot  stick  to  the  PA  surface  [56].  CNTs  are well-known for their antimicrobial effect, which in principle arises from a combination of physical and chemical mechanisms. Physical mechanisms can include integrity breaches by structural penetration in the cell walls and membranes, while chemical mechanisms can involve oxidative  stresses  due  to  interactions  with  the  biological  cells  [134,157]. These mechanisms are highly influenced by physicochemical properties of  the  CNTs such as diameter, length, electronic structure as well as surface chemistry as a result of residual catalyst, surface functionalization and coating on the CNTs [157]. In a patent disclosure by Kurth et al. [107], it was reported that partially exposed CNTs can pierce through the cell wall of microorganisms thereby leading to cell death. As such, the SWRO membrane incorporated with CNTs possess biocidal activity. A  more  negatively  charged  PA  layer  due  to  the  incorporation  of functionalized-CNTs can also reduce irreversible fouling thereby leading to lower organic fouling propensity [153].
texts.text: · Increase the hydrophilicity and surface charge of the PA layer. A more  hydrophilic  PA  layer  typically  have  higher  fouling  resistance because of lower foulant adsorption [12,120]. There has been manifold anti-fouling strategies explored based on this concept, namely, incorporating additives [71,111,155] and nanomaterials [107,127] or surface  coating  with  hydrophilic  compounds.  In  the  first  approach,  the inability of certain nanomaterials ( e.g. multiwalled CNTs) to form bonds with foulants can lead to decreased fouling propensity because hydrophobic  foulants  cannot  stick  to  the  PA  surface  [56].  CNTs  are well-known for their antimicrobial effect, which in principle arises from a combination of physical and chemical mechanisms. Physical mechanisms can include integrity breaches by structural penetration in the cell walls and membranes, while chemical mechanisms can involve oxidative  stresses  due  to  interactions  with  the  biological  cells  [134,157]. These mechanisms are highly influenced by physicochemical properties of  the  CNTs such as diameter, length, electronic structure as well as surface chemistry as a result of residual catalyst, surface functionalization and coating on the CNTs [157]. In a patent disclosure by Kurth et al. [107], it was reported that partially exposed CNTs can pierce through the cell wall of microorganisms thereby leading to cell death. As such, the SWRO membrane incorporated with CNTs possess biocidal activity. A  more  negatively  charged  PA  layer  due  to  the  incorporation  of functionalized-CNTs can also reduce irreversible fouling thereby leading to lower organic fouling propensity [153].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the surface coating approach, apart from minimizing internal fouling in the PA layer, most works focus on the hydrophilicity/surface charge enhancement of the PA backbone [145 -148]. To form a stable coating that is securely anchored to the PA layer, one of the common approaches is dip-coating [154] to generate covalent bonds between the end-groups of the coating compounds and unreacted groups of the PA layer [116,147,148]. Several SWRO membrane patents have disclosed the need to optimize the concentration of the coating compound with respect  to  the  IP  formula  to  obtain  the  best  fouling  resistance.  For example, it was reported that the precise control of the extent of reaction (between the coating compound and unreacted groups) can expose the desired  functional  groups  ( e.g. amino/PVP  [147,148],  fluorine-rich segments  [116])  at  the  outermost  surface  of  the  PA  layer,  thereby maximizing the anti-fouling property of the coating layer. Many mechanisms  have  been  proposed  to  explain  how  the  increased  hydrophilicity/surface charge can minimize the adsorption of contaminants (particularly biomaterials), namely,  the  repulsion of foulants by brush-like structures extended from PEG chains into the water phase [154], oil-repellency induced by the fluorine groups [116,147,148] or strong electrostatic interactions between zwitterions and water molecules that make the replacement of surface-bound water molecules by foulants  enthalpically  unfavorable  [156].  In  general,  there  exist  an optimal coating concentration ( e.g., 0.01 -5 wt %) such that the coating layer is not excessively thick (~0.5 -1000 nm) which can otherwise lead to severe flux decline [147] due to the additional hydrodynamic resistance  induced.  More  importantly,  the  nanomaterials/surface  coating should be robust under chemical cleaning and their effects should not weaken  over  time  [71]  to  provide  a  sustainable  long-term  fouling resistance.

To form a stable coating that is securely anchored to the PA layer, one of the common approaches is dip-coating [154] to generate covalent bonds between the end-groups of the coating compounds and unreacted groups of the PA layer [116,147,148]. Several SWRO membrane patents have disclosed the need to optimize the concentration of the coating compound with respect  to  the  IP  formula  to  obtain  the  best  fouling  resistance.  For example, it was reported that the precise control of the extent of reaction (between the coating compound and unreacted groups) can expose the desired  functional  groups  ( e.g. amino/PVP  [147,148],  fluorine-rich segments  [116])  at  the  outermost  surface  of  the  PA  layer,  thereby maximizing the anti-fouling property of the coating layer. Many mechanisms  have  been  proposed  to  explain  how  the  increased  hydrophilicity/surface charge can minimize the adsorption of contaminants (particularly biomaterials), namely,  the  repulsion of foulants by brush-like structures extended from PEG chains into the water phase [154], oil-repellency induced by the fluorine groups [116,147,148] or strong electrostatic interactions between zwitterions and water molecules that make the replacement of surface-bound water molecules by foulants  enthalpically  unfavorable  [156].  In  general,  there  exist  an optimal coating concentration ( e.g., 0.01 -5 wt %) such that the coating layer is not excessively thick (~0.5 -1000 nm) which can otherwise lead to severe flux decline [147] due to the additional hydrodynamic resistance  induced.  More  importantly,  the  nanomaterials/surface  coating should be robust under chemical cleaning and their effects should not weaken  over  time  [71]  to  provide  a  sustainable  long-term  fouling resistance.

texts.orig: 3.5. Chlorine-resistance strategies
texts.text: 3.5. Chlorine-resistance strategies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It  is  well  acknowledged  that  ring  chlorination  and  Orton  rearrangement disrupts the integrity of the polyamide which consequently leads  to  selective  layer  degradation.  In  recent  years,  many  research works  have  attempted  to  improve  the  chlorine  stability  of  SWRO membranes against oxidative degradation (refer to Table 9).

texts.orig: Multiple research works have outlined the possibility of fabricating SWRO membranes with higher chlorine resistance, with the common goal of modifying the chemistry of the selective layer. In general, these works can be summarized into four main categories:
texts.text: Multiple research works have outlined the possibility of fabricating SWRO membranes with higher chlorine resistance, with the common goal of modifying the chemistry of the selective layer. In general, these works can be summarized into four main categories:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Increase the stability of the PA layer. A common strategy adopted to increase the chlorine resistance of SWRO membranes is to increase the stability  of  the  PA  layer  by:  (1)  Removing the unreacted amine/acyl chloride groups on the PA surface, (2) introduce filler materials to form additional bonds within the PA layer and (3) increase the hydrophilicity of the PA layer. According to two patents [91,105], chlorine-resistant SWRO  membranes  can  be  fabricated  by  removing  the  residual  and unreacted monomers on the nascent PA layer via an additional reaction between unreacted monomers. This will further crosslink the PA layer and stabilize it against oxidation due to chlorination. The incorporation of filler nanomaterials such as zeolites [121], modifed silica [126] and CNTs [56] in the PA layer can strengthen the PA network with additional

texts.label: text
texts.orig: intermolecular  hydrogen  bonds,  amide  bonds  or  increase  the  crosslinking degree of the PA layer [56]. Thus, the substitution of hydrogen by chlorine on the amide groups in the PA layer will be impeded, thereby reducing the PA layer ' s susceptibility to N-chlorination. Also, increasing the hydrophilicity of the PA layer can enhance the chlorine resistance of SWRO membranes because of a stronger PA layer protection due to additional hydrogen bonding with water [12,111], thereby hindering the electrophile Cl + attack on the amide linkages. Lastly, although the mechanisms remain to be elucidated, two industrial patents have disclosed that the chlorine resistance of SWRO membranes can be achieved by increasing the roughness of the PA layer through incorporating silver nanowires [122] or by dropping TMC droplets in IP [104].
texts.text: intermolecular  hydrogen  bonds,  amide  bonds  or  increase  the  crosslinking degree of the PA layer [56]. Thus, the substitution of hydrogen by chlorine on the amide groups in the PA layer will be impeded, thereby reducing the PA layer ' s susceptibility to N-chlorination. Also, increasing the hydrophilicity of the PA layer can enhance the chlorine resistance of SWRO membranes because of a stronger PA layer protection due to additional hydrogen bonding with water [12,111], thereby hindering the electrophile Cl + attack on the amide linkages. Lastly, although the mechanisms remain to be elucidated, two industrial patents have disclosed that the chlorine resistance of SWRO membranes can be achieved by increasing the roughness of the PA layer through incorporating silver nanowires [122] or by dropping TMC droplets in IP [104].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Decrease the reactivity of PA to chlorination. In a patent disclosure by Murphy et al. [92], chlorine-resistant SWRO membranes can be obtained by introducing electron-withdrawing functional groups ( e.g. fluoro groups) onto the reaction monomers to minimize N-chlorination. Ko et al. [114] incorporated various antioxidant compounds ( e.g. phenols, amines, phosphorus and sulfur) in IP in a bid to suppress the formation of radicals under ambient conditions. It was reported that this method can increase the oxidation resistance of the PA, thereby minimizing the extent of N-chlorination and Orton rearrangement processes upon chlorine exposure. The patent also highlighted that the critical concentration of the additives was in the range of 0.02 -5 wt% and that the solubility parameters of the antioxidants used were in the range of 9 -22 (J/cm 3 ) 1/2 . This is to ensure that the permselectivity of the SWRO membrane is not compromised due to the incorporation of antioxidant compounds. It was also noticed that the compounds had bulky groups which induced steric hindrance to hamper chlorine attack. In short, the synergistic effects of electron-withdrawing and streic hindrance induced by these functional groups can minimize chlorination on the PA benzene rings  [158]  thereby  endowing  it  with  higher  chlorine  tolerance  as compared to the pristine polyamide membrane.
texts.text: · Decrease the reactivity of PA to chlorination. In a patent disclosure by Murphy et al. [92], chlorine-resistant SWRO membranes can be obtained by introducing electron-withdrawing functional groups ( e.g. fluoro groups) onto the reaction monomers to minimize N-chlorination. Ko et al. [114] incorporated various antioxidant compounds ( e.g. phenols, amines, phosphorus and sulfur) in IP in a bid to suppress the formation of radicals under ambient conditions. It was reported that this method can increase the oxidation resistance of the PA, thereby minimizing the extent of N-chlorination and Orton rearrangement processes upon chlorine exposure. The patent also highlighted that the critical concentration of the additives was in the range of 0.02 -5 wt% and that the solubility parameters of the antioxidants used were in the range of 9 -22 (J/cm 3 ) 1/2 . This is to ensure that the permselectivity of the SWRO membrane is not compromised due to the incorporation of antioxidant compounds. It was also noticed that the compounds had bulky groups which induced steric hindrance to hamper chlorine attack. In short, the synergistic effects of electron-withdrawing and streic hindrance induced by these functional groups can minimize chlorination on the PA benzene rings  [158]  thereby  endowing  it  with  higher  chlorine  tolerance  as compared to the pristine polyamide membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Introduce sacrificial functional groups onto the PA layer. The incorporation of sacrificial functional groups can shield the active amide linkages from chlorine attack if the former is intrinsically more reactive towards chlorine as compared to the latter. It is well known that aromatic polyamides are susceptible to irreversible oxidative chlorination because  of  the  electron-rich  nature  of  the  aromatic  benzene  rings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Manifold studies have shown that chlorine-resistant SWRO membranes can be fabricated by incorporating electron-rich functional groups from fluorine  compounds  [115],  zeolites  [121],  modified  silica  [126]  and CNTs [56]. Because these functional groups typically do not contain bulky aromatic groups, there is less steric hindrance for chlorination attack. Thus, it is easier for the chlorine electrophile (Cl + ) to attack these groups which consequently shield the underneath amide bonds of the PA layer from chlorine attack. In a patent disclosure by Kim et al. [115], it was  highlighted  that  the  fluorine  compounds  must  be  added  to  the aqueous phase because of the smaller amount of functional groups in the aqueous phase monomer as compared to the acyl chloride monomer. Thus, the fluorine compounds can be more easily exposed at the PA surface, acting as a sacrificial functional group to protect the PA layer from chlorine attack.
texts.text: Manifold studies have shown that chlorine-resistant SWRO membranes can be fabricated by incorporating electron-rich functional groups from fluorine  compounds  [115],  zeolites  [121],  modified  silica  [126]  and CNTs [56]. Because these functional groups typically do not contain bulky aromatic groups, there is less steric hindrance for chlorination attack. Thus, it is easier for the chlorine electrophile (Cl + ) to attack these groups which consequently shield the underneath amide bonds of the PA layer from chlorine attack. In a patent disclosure by Kim et al. [115], it was  highlighted  that  the  fluorine  compounds  must  be  added  to  the aqueous phase because of the smaller amount of functional groups in the aqueous phase monomer as compared to the acyl chloride monomer. Thus, the fluorine compounds can be more easily exposed at the PA surface, acting as a sacrificial functional group to protect the PA layer from chlorine attack.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · Surface coating with chemical compounds. Manifold studies also outlined  the  possibility  of  fabricating  chlorine-resistant  SWRO  membranes  by  coating  the  PA  layer  with  crosslinking  monomers  such  as hydroxyl groups. It was hypothesized that the hydrophilic copolymers can  encourage  intermolecular  hydrogen  bonding  in  the  polyamide linkages which will hinder the chlorination and hydrolysis reactions on the amide linkages. In a patent disclosure by Lee et al. [145], it was reported that chlorine resistant SWRO membranes can be fabricated by coating  the  PA  surface  with  a  hydrophilic  layer  comprising  complex metal oxides. The additional chemical bonding between the polyamide and  hydrophilic  coating  protected  the  PA  layer  from  degradation because chlorine attack is hindered. The patent also disclosed that the coating need not fully cover the entire PA layer and that a prolonged coating time will lead to non-uniform coating on the PA layer.

Manifold studies also outlined  the  possibility  of  fabricating  chlorine-resistant  SWRO  membranes  by  coating  the  PA  layer  with  crosslinking  monomers  such  as hydroxyl groups. It was hypothesized that the hydrophilic copolymers can  encourage  intermolecular  hydrogen  bonding  in  the  polyamide linkages which will hinder the chlorination and hydrolysis reactions on the amide linkages. In a patent disclosure by Lee et al. [145], it was reported that chlorine resistant SWRO membranes can be fabricated by coating  the  PA  surface  with  a  hydrophilic  layer  comprising  complex metal oxides. The additional chemical bonding between the polyamide and  hydrophilic  coating  protected  the  PA  layer  from  degradation because chlorine attack is hindered. The patent also disclosed that the coating need not fully cover the entire PA layer and that a prolonged coating time will lead to non-uniform coating on the PA layer.

texts.orig: The industrial SWRO manufacturers have been the main contributor driving the development of innovative SWRO membranes that are more energy  efficient  and  productive.  The  key  industrial  SWRO  manufacturers are listed in Table 10 together with the performance of the latest state-of-the-art  SWRO  membranes  currently  available  in  the  market. These  SWRO  spiral-wound  membrane  elements  have  been  widely deployed in various seawater desalination plants worldwide (Table 10) which reaffirms their potential to survive in real world conditions. The prevalent  membrane structure  adopted  by  the  SWRO  industry  is  the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: TFC-PA type [5] because of its ease of scale-up, versatility and stable performance. The only exception are the TFN membranes manufactured by LG Chemicals, which comprises proprietary filler materials incorporated into the PA selective layer [36]. However, there is no further information by LG Chemicals with regards to the type and location of the filler  material  in  the  PA  layer.  Nano-scale  characterization  of  the state-of-the-art industrial TFC-SWRO membranes revealed that the PA layer characteristics did not differ much from conventional RO membranes. As shown in Fig. 15, these industrial SWRO membranes possess the classical ridge and valley surface morphology that is characteristic of the fully aromatic polyamide made from MPD-TMC chemistry. Also, the PA layers were relatively tall (250 -300 nm in height) with a roughness average  ( Ra )  of  around  50 -90  nm  (Fig.  15).  The  PA  layers  are  also relatively  hydrophobic  with  contact  angles  of  60-80 o (Fig.  15).  It  is postulated that the PA layer must be sufficiently thick and dense (at least 200 nm in terms of height) to enable the SWRO membranes to attain the high  salt  rejection  ( ≥ 99.8%)  under  high  salinity  conditions  (32,000 mg/L NaCl). More importantly, the PA layer cannot be too thin in order to  ensure  its  robustness  against  the  harsh  conditions  of  SWRO  with applied pressures up to 83 bar [30 -32].

More importantly, the PA layer cannot be too thin in order to  ensure  its  robustness  against  the  harsh  conditions  of  SWRO  with applied pressures up to 83 bar [30 -32].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to the mature key players, some industrial companies have demonstrated other alternatives in the field of SWRO membrane fabrication as evidenced by the numerous patents summarized in the earlier  sections.  The  key  players  of  the  SWRO  industrial  patents  are summarized in the form of representative patents of each company in Table 11. It is worth to note that although many alternatives ( e.g. TFNSWRO membranes) exist in the industry, they do not exhibit the potential to replace the state-of-the-art TFC SWRO membranes currently used in the industry. For example, the SWRO patents summarized in the earlier  sections  were  predominantly  tested  in  small  coupon  scales (60 -140 cm 2 ) [112,123], whereas the effective permeation area of an actual SWRO spiral-wound element is ~40 m 2 , which is several orders of magnitude larger. Hence, the ability to scale up these next-generation SWRO membranes is something that needs affirmation. Unless these next-generation SWRO membranes can be scaled up to element size and is proven to have the chemical stability and mechanical robustness to survive the harsh conditions of SWRO [167], the current TFC-SWRO membranes used in the industries will likely remain as the gold standard for SWRO desalination.
texts.text: In addition to the mature key players, some industrial companies have demonstrated other alternatives in the field of SWRO membrane fabrication as evidenced by the numerous patents summarized in the earlier  sections.  The  key  players  of  the  SWRO  industrial  patents  are summarized in the form of representative patents of each company in Table 11. It is worth to note that although many alternatives ( e.g. TFNSWRO membranes) exist in the industry, they do not exhibit the potential to replace the state-of-the-art TFC SWRO membranes currently used in the industry. For example, the SWRO patents summarized in the earlier  sections  were  predominantly  tested  in  small  coupon  scales (60 -140 cm 2 ) [112,123], whereas the effective permeation area of an actual SWRO spiral-wound element is ~40 m 2 , which is several orders of magnitude larger. Hence, the ability to scale up these next-generation SWRO membranes is something that needs affirmation. Unless these next-generation SWRO membranes can be scaled up to element size and is proven to have the chemical stability and mechanical robustness to survive the harsh conditions of SWRO [167], the current TFC-SWRO membranes used in the industries will likely remain as the gold standard for SWRO desalination.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: industry have developed new advanced low pressure SWRO membranes that has the potential to lower the energy consumption of SWRO. When compared against the conventional SWRO membranes, the low pressure SWRO membranes can either i) attain the same water flux using a lower applied pressure or ii) attain higher water flux under the same applied pressure. According to the data presented in Table 10, it is noticed that the  membrane  permeability  of  SWRO  membranes  has  gradually increased  at  a  constant  salt  rejection  of  99.8%.  For  example,  the Hydranautics SWC6-LD membrane can attain 37% higher permeability as  compared  to  its  predecessor  (SWC5-LD)  [31].  Empirical  evidence have also shown that the low pressure SWRO membranes developed by Toray Industries can reduce the operating pressure of SWRO by 15% (from 60 bar to 50 bar) at a fixed permeate flux [5]. The advancement and  development  of  SWRO  membranes  are  presented  in  terms  of  a milestone chart in Fig. 16, using prominent examples from the industrial SWRO membrane key players. In short, the latest products from the SWRO membrane industries can achieve high selectivity (99.8% salt rejection) while demonstrating the ability to produce the same quantity of freshwater using a lower operating pressure. Because the operating pressure of SWRO is very high (50 -80 bar), a small decrease in pressure requirement can lead to huge energy savings.

texts.text: 4. Perspectives and future outlook

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Distinctions of TFC membranes for SWRO and BWRO/NF
texts.text: 4.1. Distinctions of TFC membranes for SWRO and BWRO/NF

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Throughout this review, we have constantly been drawing comparison to TFC membranes for BWRO and NF processes for a simple reason -the membrane requirements to handle seawater as compared to brackish water or industrial wastewaters are distinctly different owing to the very different  nature  of  the  waters.  As  evidenced  by  Table  12,  seawater consist up to > 400 times more NaCl salt content than brackish water, including other substances that severely increase the fouling propensity of  seawater.  Besides,  the  required  quality  for  permeate  water  from SWRO is also the most stringent (Table 12). This entails that SWRO membranes handle the most challenging water and yet must produce the purest water. For these reasons, membrane specifications for SWRO are much more demanding. Moving forward, it is therefore important that the reader has a common understanding on the key differences between TFC membranes for SWRO and BWRO/NF processes.

texts.orig: these strategies, such PA selective layer would be of little relevance to SWRO given the lack of mechanical strength to withstand the high hydraulic pressures used and also the higher salt permeability that arises as a result of permselective trade-off. Experience suggests that a PA active layer  for  SWRO  should  have  a  large  enough  height  in  the  range  of 150 -300 nm to ensure intact membrane integrity as well as structural robustness.  The  crosslinking  degree  should  also  be  correspondingly higher than BWRO and NF membranes to elevate rejection capacity to the  required  99.8%,  which  remains  an  uncompromisable  target  for SWRO.

Experience suggests that a PA active layer  for  SWRO  should  have  a  large  enough  height  in  the  range  of 150 -300 nm to ensure intact membrane integrity as well as structural robustness.  The  crosslinking  degree  should  also  be  correspondingly higher than BWRO and NF membranes to elevate rejection capacity to the  required  99.8%,  which  remains  an  uncompromisable  target  for SWRO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To handle seawater, the support layer for SWRO membrane must also be different. Particularly, the membrane structure of the support must  be  sufficiently  dense,  either  of  a  fully  or  partially  sponge-like structure,  to  offer  a  strong  sublayer  that  can  withstand  the  high compressive stress brought by the hydraulic pressure. Surface pores and roughness should also be carefully optimized. As aforementioned, there is no room for any impairment on the PA selective layer as, unlike in BWRO and NF where minor defects remain tolerable, any defects will potentially cause the TFC membrane to malfunction under the harsh operating  conditions  of  SWRO.  Hence,  there  are  some  strict  prerequisites on the surface pore sizes and roughness, in addition to surface hydrophilicity of the support layer, to ensure an optimized IP. Again, from experience, we suggest that the pore size should ideally be tuned within  10 -70 nm for forming an excellent PA  selective layer  that  is flawless and robustly stable for SWRO.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Upper bound for SWRO membranes
texts.text: 4.2. Upper bound for SWRO membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In one of the most comprehensive surveys to date covering all SWRO, BWRO and NF processes, Yang et al. recently defined an upper bound limit for TFC membranes based on BWRO data [38]. Herein, we have decided to deconstruct the BWRO data and recreate a new upper bound specifically for SWRO membranes. Our rationale for doing so stems from the following reasons: (1) An upper bound limit defined by BWRO data will not be useful for SWRO given that TFC membrane design for BWRO are uniquely different -SWRO feed is relatively more challenging to handle (see Section 4.1 and Table 12). (2) All previous upper bounds

Our rationale for doing so stems from the following reasons: (1) An upper bound limit defined by BWRO data will not be useful for SWRO given that TFC membrane design for BWRO are uniquely different -SWRO feed is relatively more challenging to handle (see Section 4.1 and Table 12). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our new upper bound features a new limit based entirely on SWRO data. In addition, we have added TFC membranes performance results from patents to supplement those from commercial TFC membranes and the literature to provide the reader with the most complete collation of data for an accurate definition of the upper bound limit centered on the SWRO process. All the empirical raw data (sample size: 105) used for constructing the new upper bound is as summarized in Fig. 17A. Most recent works by Yang et al. and Werber et al. have reported their upper bounds in water-salt permselectivity, A/B , against water permeability coefficient, A , given that the evaluation of the TFC membranes is simpler without the need to ascertain the height of the PA active layer [38,138]. Hence, we have plotted our data in identical fashion and showcased the new upper bound as shown in Fig. 17B. The new upper bound for SWRO demonstrates an unequivocal tradeoff, similar to those reported for TFC membranes for RO and NF processes. And its limit is defined mainly by data from patents, which justifies why we want to expand our upper bound  in  the  first  place  to  include  patents  amidst  data  points  from commercial membranes and the literature. A stark difference between our upper bound as compared to others is the significantly lower limit, suggesting that performance benchmarking of TFC membranes is more befitting for SWRO (Fig. 17B).

In addition, we have added TFC membranes performance results from patents to supplement those from commercial TFC membranes and the literature to provide the reader with the most complete collation of data for an accurate definition of the upper bound limit centered on the SWRO process. All the empirical raw data (sample size: 105) used for constructing the new upper bound is as summarized in Fig. 17A. Most recent works by Yang et al. and Werber et al. have reported their upper bounds in water-salt permselectivity, A/B , against water permeability coefficient, A , given that the evaluation of the TFC membranes is simpler without the need to ascertain the height of the PA active layer [38,138]. Hence, we have plotted our data in identical fashion and showcased the new upper bound as shown in Fig. 17B. The new upper bound for SWRO demonstrates an unequivocal tradeoff, similar to those reported for TFC membranes for RO and NF processes. And its limit is defined mainly by data from patents, which justifies why we want to expand our upper bound  in  the  first  place  to  include  patents  amidst  data  points  from commercial membranes and the literature. A stark difference between our upper bound as compared to others is the significantly lower limit, suggesting that performance benchmarking of TFC membranes is more befitting for SWRO (Fig. 17B).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore,  we  have  also  constructed  upper  bounds  for  SWRO membranes after membrane fouling and chlorine attack as well as for boron rejection (Fig. 18A -C). Unsurprisingly, the limits of these upper bounds are lower than the SWRO upper bound limit as demonstrated in Fig. 17B, once again exposing the current inadequacy of SWRO membranes in dealing with detrimental effects brought by fouling, chlorine and boron. Nevertheless, analogous to SWRO upper bound that is good for benchmarking next-generation TFC membranes, these upper bound limits  will  furnish  functional  benchmarking  of  SWRO  performances from  a  true  practical  perspective.  To  apply  these  upper  bounds  as benchmarking tools, there are several cases worth highlighting. Case I is a hypothetical situation where SWRO performances ideally surpass that of the upper bound limit (Fig. 18D). This highly desired case will call for TFC-SWRO membranes to exceed expectations by performing beyond current state-of-the-art membranes. Following that, case II demonstrates an improvement by moving towards the direction of the upper bound limit (Fig. 18D and E) which suggests that the strategies adopted are compelling for performance enhancements. For case III, the improvements move parallel to the upper bound limit (Fig. 18F). This indicates a permselective  tradeoff -either  increasing  water  permeability  at  the expense of water-salt permselectivity or vice versa -which probably entails poor SWRO membrane fabrication strategies. And, if case IV occurs where  the  trend  moves  away  from  the  upper  bound  (Fig.  18F),  the strategies  are  likely  proven  ineffectual  unless  there  are  other  valueadding attributes such as anti-fouling or chlorine resistant effect that justify the drop in membrane performances. On the basis of these four potential  cases,  it  is  therefore  imperative  that  any  membrane  design optimization and modification efforts be measured against a reliable standard  to  provide  positive  feedback  to  researchers  and  help  them embrace a data-driven approach for the future design and fabrication of TFC-SWRO membranes.

Unsurprisingly, the limits of these upper bounds are lower than the SWRO upper bound limit as demonstrated in Fig. 17B, once again exposing the current inadequacy of SWRO membranes in dealing with detrimental effects brought by fouling, chlorine and boron. Case I is a hypothetical situation where SWRO performances ideally surpass that of the upper bound limit (Fig. 18D). This highly desired case will call for TFC-SWRO membranes to exceed expectations by performing beyond current state-of-the-art membranes. Following that, case II demonstrates an improvement by moving towards the direction of the upper bound limit (Fig. 18D and E) which suggests that the strategies adopted are compelling for performance enhancements. For case III, the improvements move parallel to the upper bound limit (Fig. 18F). This indicates a permselective  tradeoff -either  increasing  water  permeability  at  the expense of water-salt permselectivity or vice versa -which probably entails poor SWRO membrane fabrication strategies. And, if case IV occurs where  the  trend  moves  away  from  the  upper  bound  (Fig.  18F),  the strategies  are  likely  proven  ineffectual  unless  there  are  other  valueadding attributes such as anti-fouling or chlorine resistant effect that justify the drop in membrane performances. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Next-generation SWRO membranes
texts.text: 4.3. Next-generation SWRO membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With a clear understanding of the uniqueness of SWRO membranes, we are in a better position to discuss the strategies needed to design nextgeneration  TFC  membranes  for  SWRO.  The  next-generation  SWRO membranes should target the four Achilles heels of SWRO membranes discussed thus far, which encompass not only high performances, but also high capacity to elevate boron rejection, and strengthen resistance towards fouling and chlorine. A myriad of strategies used by academics in the literature, ranging from tuning IP formulae, optimizing support layer structures, surface modifications, to leveraging porous filler materials and biological water channels, have already been reviewed and discussed in Section 3. To further add value to these efforts, we put current limitations of SWRO membranes into perspective and provide key recommendations from the membrane standpoint.
texts.text: With a clear understanding of the uniqueness of SWRO membranes, we are in a better position to discuss the strategies needed to design nextgeneration  TFC  membranes  for  SWRO.  The  next-generation  SWRO membranes should target the four Achilles heels of SWRO membranes discussed thus far, which encompass not only high performances, but also high capacity to elevate boron rejection, and strengthen resistance towards fouling and chlorine. A myriad of strategies used by academics in the literature, ranging from tuning IP formulae, optimizing support layer structures, surface modifications, to leveraging porous filler materials and biological water channels, have already been reviewed and discussed in Section 3. To further add value to these efforts, we put current limitations of SWRO membranes into perspective and provide key recommendations from the membrane standpoint.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We envision a technology roadmap for the next-generation membranes (Fig. 19), integrating functional designs for practical solutions to alleviate  the  Achilles  heels  of  SWRO  membranes.  As  shown  in  the roadmap, we highlight to the reader the strategies which we feel will create impact in the short term for SWRO, and those that will require a longer term duration. Firstly, the use of filler (nano)materials to realize high-performance,  high  boron  rejection,  anti-fouling,  and  chlorine resistance TFN membranes is an attractive approach commonly adopted in the literature. While a breakthrough in materials for SWRO is definitely a quantum leap, there are by no means a lack of novel materials to try.  Novel  materials,  such  as  carbon-based  nanomaterials,  MOFs, covalent-organic frameworks (COFs), zeolites, and MXenes ( i.e. metal carbides, nitrides or carbonitrides) have demonstrated promising results for SWRO [134,172,173]. However, there remains few TFN membranes that can reach a NaCl rejection capacity of 99.8%. We attribute this to potential problems, such as materials dispersion inhomogeneity, poor particle size distribution control, compatibility issues with the PA matrix, and the lack of understanding of the roles played by the materials, which result in the formation of a PA selective layer that is deficient in handling  seawater.  Hence,  we  consider  the  scouting  of  cutting-edge

texts.orig: As  for  now,  efforts  with  immediate  impacts  for  TFN  membranes should instead focus on resolving the above problems with current filler materials as well as looking into the following: (1) long-term performance and mechanical stability of the TFN membranes, (2) hydrolytic stability of the materials, (3) leaching and toxicity studies to understand the impacts of the materials on human health and the environment, and (4) translational challenges with scaling up laboratory-to commercialscale  fabrication.  Notably,  the  capital  costs  of  producing  these  TFN membranes are not going to be attractive, owing to extra costs incurred from incorporating these materials. Many of these materials are not even commercially available. This means that their cost prices are expectedly higher. Hence, despite the fact that the resulting TFN membranes can exhibit enhanced separation performances and produce energy savings during  SWRO operations,  the  return  of  investment  will  need  careful evaluations. Also, the cost-competitiveness of TFN membranes necessitates  detailed  techno-economic  reviews  to  understand  the  economic feasibility  of  this  strategy.  Without  de-risking  all  these  underlying problems, the use of TFN membranes for SWRO is unlikely to get buy-in from the industry and prospective investors.
