texts.orig: Secondly, membrane fouling is a flux-driven process. This means that ultrapermeable membranes are more susceptible to fouling, which can negate the benefits of high flux. Fouling is also an inevitable process, which makes the use of chlorine to control biofouling necessary. However, commercial TFC membranes are prone to chlorine attack, veering the process of SWRO into a vicious cycle between fouling control and chlorine  susceptibility.  Recently,  there  is  some  empirical  evidence showing that the dosing of chlorine and sodium bisulfate (SBS) triggers biofouling [5]. Hence, it is forecasted that SWRO will eventually move towards green desalination with SWRO operation involving less chemicals as well as a lower frequency of chemical cleaning to reduce the associated environmental impacts [5]. Nevertheless, before such a green approach  is  being  practised  in  SWRO  operation,  we  reckon  that  the development of intrinsically anti-fouling and chlorine resistant membranes via incorporating filler materials is going to be a long-term goal for reasons already discussed. Other strategies, such as surface modifications and tuning of IP formulae to optimize surface properties, can be realized within a short- or mid-term duration, owing to their simplicity as well as facile integration into existing TFC membrane production.
texts.text: Secondly, membrane fouling is a flux-driven process. This means that ultrapermeable membranes are more susceptible to fouling, which can negate the benefits of high flux. Fouling is also an inevitable process, which makes the use of chlorine to control biofouling necessary. However, commercial TFC membranes are prone to chlorine attack, veering the process of SWRO into a vicious cycle between fouling control and chlorine  susceptibility.  Recently,  there  is  some  empirical  evidence showing that the dosing of chlorine and sodium bisulfate (SBS) triggers biofouling [5]. Hence, it is forecasted that SWRO will eventually move towards green desalination with SWRO operation involving less chemicals as well as a lower frequency of chemical cleaning to reduce the associated environmental impacts [5]. Nevertheless, before such a green approach  is  being  practised  in  SWRO  operation,  we  reckon  that  the development of intrinsically anti-fouling and chlorine resistant membranes via incorporating filler materials is going to be a long-term goal for reasons already discussed. Other strategies, such as surface modifications and tuning of IP formulae to optimize surface properties, can be realized within a short- or mid-term duration, owing to their simplicity as well as facile integration into existing TFC membrane production.

texts.orig: perturbation and shear stress at the membrane surface. The strategy is non-membrane related and thus will not be elaborated here (interested readers  can  refer  to  dedicated  review  [174]  for  more  information). Nevertheless, these techniques are also already matured for low pressure hollow fiber modules for microfiltration (MF) and ultrafiltration (UF) systems. Yet, exploiting them on flat-sheet TFC spiral wound modules that are common for SWRO remains challenging. Hollow fiber membranes  and  their  modules  have  been  successfully  applied  to  many membrane processes such as MF, UF, NF, forward osmosis (FO) and BWRO [174], owing to their self-supporting structure, larger membrane-area-per-unit-module-volume  packing  density,  as  well  as higher energy efficiency within-module mixing, which makes it advantages for fouling mitigation [175]. Hence, hollow fiber membranes are theoretically a viable option for SWRO. However, a myriad of challenges remains to be resolved. These challenges include optimizing the selective  layer  on  a  hollow  fiber  configuration  to  meet  the  stringent requirement of SWRO, fabricating hollow fiber substrates with excellent mechanical properties to withstand the high transmembrane pressure needed for SWRO, and overcoming the undesirable pressure drop in the lumen side of the hollow fiber membranes. Currently, there is only one commercial SWRO hollow fiber membrane module by Toyobo Co., Ltd., based on cellulose triacetate. The fibers are designed with a small internal surface area so as to withstand the high-pressure conditions in SWRO. Because of this design, the hollow fibers have a very low water permeability ( < 0.5 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 ) with NaCl rejection and net pressure  driving  force  of  the  module  far  below  that  of  TFC  flat-sheet

texts.orig: Fig. 19. Technology roadmap of the SWRO membrane  fabrication, showing different short-  and  long-term  strategies.  First  layer (in  grey)  represents  the  challenges  facing SWRO membranes, while  the  second  layer onwards  represents  the  long-to  short-term strategies that are current put in place. These strategies are categorized into three groups: membranes (in red circles), fabrication and modification  methodologies  (in  yellow  circles) and other relevant non-membrane strategies (in green circles), which we would like to recognize here. The size of the circle represents the impacts they are likely to  make  towards  next-generation  SWRO membranes.  (For  interpretation  of  the  references  to  color  in  this  figure  legend,  the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

texts.orig: membranes [12]. Hence, we argue that there is a need to focus on the fabrication of hollow fibers and close the aforementioned gaps so that SWRO hollow fibers can increase our capacity to deal with (bio)fouling. Flat-sheet  spiral  wound membrane element has been dominating the SWRO market for the past 50 years and is the state-of-the-art membrane adopted by most seawater desalination plants worldwide. To the best of our knowledge, the sole hollow fiber SWRO membranes by Toyobo Co., Ltd. is only deployed in Saudi Arabia [176]. The eventual deployment of hollow  fibers  in  SWRO  would  therefore  necessitates  a  technological breakthrough to produce attractive fibers that are mechanically comparable to the flat-sheet counterparts and can withstand the high applied pressure of the SWRO process. Before that happens, a short-term strategy is to develop alternative disinfectants and biocides that have minimal effect on the PA selective layer. Together, these strategies can help reduce our dependency on chlorine resistant membranes.

Hence, we argue that there is a need to focus on the fabrication of hollow fibers and close the aforementioned gaps so that SWRO hollow fibers can increase our capacity to deal with (bio)fouling. Flat-sheet  spiral  wound membrane element has been dominating the SWRO market for the past 50 years and is the state-of-the-art membrane adopted by most seawater desalination plants worldwide. To the best of our knowledge, the sole hollow fiber SWRO membranes by Toyobo Co., Ltd. is only deployed in Saudi Arabia [176]. The eventual deployment of hollow  fibers  in  SWRO  would  therefore  necessitates  a  technological breakthrough to produce attractive fibers that are mechanically comparable to the flat-sheet counterparts and can withstand the high applied pressure of the SWRO process. Before that happens, a short-term strategy is to develop alternative disinfectants and biocides that have minimal effect on the PA selective layer. Together, these strategies can help reduce our dependency on chlorine resistant membranes.

texts.orig: From the viewpoint of the SWRO membrane industry, there remains a  need to reduce the energy consumption of SWRO further. Because SWRO is an energy-intensive process, the impact of a slight reduction in energy consumption will be tremendous on the cumulative scale [55]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: performance SWRO  membranes  that can give the same water throughput at a lower operating pressure [5]. For the SWRO system, monitoring  the  SWRO  feed  using  membrane  biofilm  formation  rate (mBFR) is necessary for reliable operation of SWRO plants. Empirical evidence has also shown that chlorine is ineffective and counterproductive in the sterilization of marine bacteria [5]. Thus, we believe that future SWRO systems should involve less chemical usage ( e.g. chlorine), but instead optimize the pre-treatment process to provide accurate and timely feed water characteristics by mBFR monitoring [5]. We will not further discuss it as it is beyond the scope of the current review. For the SWRO membrane unit, innovative low pressure SWRO membranes are desired. More importantly, the novel SWRO membrane should have low fouling  propensity  to  ensure  its  high  performance  in  the  long-term. Because thermodynamics has set a limit on the energy savings achievable  by  the  membrane  itself,  future  innovative  SWRO  membranes should have a permeability of at most ~2 -3 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 but with as high as possible selectivity ( ≥ 99.8% salt rejection) in order to minimize the energy consumption of SWRO.

We will not further discuss it as it is beyond the scope of the current review. For the SWRO membrane unit, innovative low pressure SWRO membranes are desired. More importantly, the novel SWRO membrane should have low fouling  propensity  to  ensure  its  high  performance  in  the  long-term. Because thermodynamics has set a limit on the energy savings achievable  by  the  membrane  itself,  future  innovative  SWRO  membranes should have a permeability of at most ~2 -3 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 but with as high as possible selectivity ( ≥ 99.8% salt rejection) in order to minimize the energy consumption of SWRO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Overall, from an overarching perspective, we assemble the strategies discussed in Section 3 and put them into the following three aspects: (1) permselectivity enhancement, (2) commercial viability that encompass industrial  relevance,  scalability  and  mechanical  robustness  and  (3) membrane  production  cost  (Fig.  20).  Apparently,  next-generation membranes  such  as  vertically  aligned  carbon  nanotube  and  singlelayer  graphene  membranes stand at the top of the plot with highest possible  performance  enhancement  and  production  costs  but  lowest commercial  viability.  Comparatively,  conventional  TFC  polymeric membranes (based on the MPD-TMC chemistry) take the opposite end. TFN and biomimetic membranes, which are to date demonstrated in journals and patents, are likely to take the center of the plot given their higher technical difficulties in scaling up the fabrication process and the absence  of  thorough  techno-economic  analysis.  Obviously,  newly developed TFC membranes through the incorporation of additives or surface  modifications  will  lie  between  the  biomimetic/TFN  and  conventional TFC membranes, owing to the fact that their fabrications will involve  only  minor  tweaks  in  the  current  commercial  fabrication process.
texts.text: Overall, from an overarching perspective, we assemble the strategies discussed in Section 3 and put them into the following three aspects: (1) permselectivity enhancement, (2) commercial viability that encompass industrial  relevance,  scalability  and  mechanical  robustness  and  (3) membrane  production  cost  (Fig.  20).  Apparently,  next-generation membranes  such  as  vertically  aligned  carbon  nanotube  and  singlelayer  graphene  membranes stand at the top of the plot with highest possible  performance  enhancement  and  production  costs  but  lowest commercial  viability.  Comparatively,  conventional  TFC  polymeric membranes (based on the MPD-TMC chemistry) take the opposite end. TFN and biomimetic membranes, which are to date demonstrated in journals and patents, are likely to take the center of the plot given their higher technical difficulties in scaling up the fabrication process and the absence  of  thorough  techno-economic  analysis.  Obviously,  newly developed TFC membranes through the incorporation of additives or surface  modifications  will  lie  between  the  biomimetic/TFN  and  conventional TFC membranes, owing to the fact that their fabrications will involve  only  minor  tweaks  in  the  current  commercial  fabrication process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Concluding remarks
texts.text: 5. Concluding remarks

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, we have reviewed recent progress in TFC membrane development  for  SWRO,  focusing  on  the  four  main  Achilles  heels, namely permselective tradeoff, insufficient single-pass boron rejection, membrane fouling and poor chlorine resistance. Membrane design and fabrication strategies to optimize support and selective layers of TFC membranes for improving membrane performances are evaluated, and surface-centric strategies to modify PA active layers for mitigating poor boron  rejection,  membrane  fouling  and  chlorine  resistance  are  discussed. The current status of the SWRO membrane industry and key needs are also laid out. SWRO will likely remain as the most efficient technology  for  seawater  desalination.  Moving  forward,  we  need  to discriminate TFC membrane designs that are unique to SWRO, owing to the different challenges faced in processing SWRO feed. This will allow us to better single-out effective strategies to improve TFC membrane performances for SWRO. Next, an upper bound constructed entirely on SWRO  membrane  performance  data  to  help  the  reader  benchmarks prospective TFC-SWRO membranes are proposed. We envision future directions  to  realize  next-generation  TFC  membranes  for  SWRO.  A roadmap illustrating key long- and short-term strategies is presented. TFN membranes incorporating advanced materials are long-term endeavors  with  critical  challenges  yet-to-be  resolved  while  developing SWRO hollow fiber membranes are deemed strategic for better (bio) fouling  control.  Overall,  by  initiating  stronger  efforts  in  elevating membrane materials and fabrication methods to address these Achilles heels of SWRO, we are in a better position to say that next-generation SWRO membranes will be more competitive in meeting the growing
